1Bqy62ftiJSlWS5zI9YGPPk-NJvYB4tN_.text: AVRIL 2026 1 Alabuga, nœud central de la menace hybride russe Contournement de Sanctions, Augmentation de la Production, Élargissement et Militarisation des Installations et Plus de Main-d'œuvre Étrangère AVRIL 2026 2 Points Clés Le conflit en Ukraine a constitué la première guerre majeure marquée par l'utilisation généralisée de drones d'attaque à usage unique ; le modèle russe Geran-2 — dérivé du Shahed-136 iranien — y est employé pour cibler les infrastructures civiles et militaires ukrainiennes sur une base quasi quotidienne. Le dernier trimestre de 2024 a enregistré un nombre d'attaques de drones supérieur à celui des 23 mois précédents réunis — une tendance qui s'est poursuivie en 2025, le rythme atteignant plus de 6 000 attaques par mois au cours du second semestre de l'année. Cette augmentation spectaculaire a été rendue possible par l'expansion significative des installations de production au sein de la Zone économique spéciale (ZES) d'Alabuga, située au Tatarstan (Russie). Le site d'Alabuga a fait l'objet d'un développement majeur entre la fin de 2024 et 2025 : extension des installations de production de drones, création de nouveaux espaces d'hébergement pour accueillir une main-d'œuvre élargie, ainsi que renforcement progressif de la fortification du site par des systèmes de défense antiaérienne et d'autres mesures de sécurité. Avec l'achèvement de quatre complexes résidentiels, Alabuga a développé une capacité d'accueil estimée à 71 000 travailleurs, ce qui représenterait une augmentation considérable des effectifs et de la capacité de production. En collaboration avec l'État russe, la ZES d'Alabuga s'est efforcée d'attirer des citoyens russes patriotes pour travailler sur ses installations. Toutefois, l'initiative la plus controversée réside dans l'effort concerté visant à recruter des ressortissants étrangers par le biais de la campagne « Alabuga Start ». Présentés comme une opportunité de mobilité professionnelle en Russie, les supports marketing léchés d'Alabuga dissimulent la nature militaro-industrielle des activités proposées aux recrues — principalement de jeunes femmes issues des pays du Sud. Ces recrues internationales ont également été complétées par des travailleurs fournis dans le cadre d'accords interétatiques conclus avec des alliés historiques — notamment prétendument entre 7 000 et 25 000 Nord-Coréens, selon certaines estimations — soulevant ainsi des inquiétudes quant à l'éventualité de recours au travail forcé. Si certains États, tels que la Corée du Nord, peuvent agir en tant que complices volontaires au sein de cette machine militaire — et si les citoyens russes y participent par devoir patriotique — les recrues internationales peuvent, quant à elles, ignorer qu'elles sont entraînées au cœur du nouveau complexe militaro-industriel du Kremlin. Ce rapport examine le développement, les opérations et les stratégies de recrutement à Alabuga, site qui constitue une étude de cas illustrant la collaboration militaire, technologique et en matière de ressources humaines entre la Russie, l'Iran, la Chine et la Corée du Nord — une coopération qui a rendu possible la guerre en Ukraine et qui fait peser des risques bien au-delà de ce conflit. AVRIL 2026 3 Une architecture industrielle en pleine expansion Le complexe d'Alabuga est implanté dans la Zone Économique Spéciale de la République du Tatarstan, à proximité immédiate de la ville d'Alabuga. Il s'agit d'un « campus industriel » fermé, placé sous la gouvernance conjointe de JSC Alabuga SEZ, des autorités du Tatarstan et de Moscou, avec une forte implication du Ministère russe de la Défense. Deux bâtiments principaux concentrent la production de drones : ● Bâtiment 8.1 (Est) : Production partiellement dédiée aux drones Geran-2, le reste servant à d'autres projets industriels. ● Bâtiment 8.2 (Ouest) : Entièrement dédié à l'assemblage des Geran-2. L'analyse d'images satellitaires de septembre 2023 révèle une moindre accumulation de neige sur les toits de ces deux bâtiments par rapport aux structures voisines, signe d'une activité industrielle continue et d'un chauffage significatif, confirmant leur utilisation intensive. Superposition de la vue satellite et du plan d’implantation interne pour la production des drones. Source AVRIL 2026 4 Le complexe est en travaux depuis début 2025 pour voir une extension de l’outil de production et des logements dédiés aux travailleurs. Ensemble de vues satellite montrant l’expansion du complexe de production de drones à Alabuga et les zones de logements dédiées aux travailleurs étrangers. Source: Sentinel-2 Zoom sur les nouvelles zones de logements. Source: t.me/DniproOfficial Entre fin 2024 et juillet 2025, au moins 8 nouvelles structures de type entrepôt/atelier ont été identifiées par imagerie satellitaire à proximité des bâtiments de production des drones. 240 nouveaux bâtiments résidentiels modulaires préfabriqués sont en construction durant cette période, pour une capacité d'accueil estimée à 41 000 ouvriers. Une ville de tentes temporaire de 119 tentes (camp « Alabuga Build ») a été montée en quelques semaines début juillet 2025 près des chaînes de production, avec 2 500 étudiants mobilisés par les « Brigades Étudiantes Russes ». AVRIL 2026 5 Vue satellite du complexe de production des drones Geran-2 à Alabuga au 06 février 2026. Source image : VANDOR; Analyse : INPACT Le 06 février 2026, il est possible d’observer une nouvelle unité de production et cinq zones de logements des travailleurs assemblant les drones. Pour mieux soutenir les activités de production, un nouveau hub de transport a été construit, fournissant des liaisons ferroviaires directes entre la Russie et la Chine, prévue pour transporter jusqu'à 100.000 conteneurs par an. En complément des vols réguliers depuis les usines chinoises vers le Tatarstan. Une montée en puissance de la production La Russie a établi ses ateliers afin de pouvoir monter rapidement en production, tout en modernisant le modèle initial copié du Shahed-136. Les Russes ont augmenté l'ogive du drone à 90 kg, remplaçant les charges standard par des charges thermobariques et incendiaires. Grâce à des antennes anti-bruit, le drone est capable d'ignorer les systèmes de brouillage ukrainien, et les dernières modifications intègrent des caméras vidéo en haute définition et des modems 4G pour corriger le vol grâce à l’usage de cartes SIM des opérateurs GSM ukrainiens ou polonais notamment. Le coût de ces drones est de 30 à 50 000 USD. AVRIL 2026 6 Pour permettre cette monté capacitaire, les cadences de production ont connu une accélération spectaculaire : ● Fin 2023 : 20 drones Geran-2 produits par jour (2 équipes), soit environ 600/mois. ● Avril 2024 : 4 500 unités livrées au Ministère de la Défense. ● Mai 2025 : cadence de 2 700 Geran-2 et 2 500 Gerbera (drones leurres) par mois, soit plus de 5 000 appareils mensuels.1 ● Juillet 2025 : 6 100 Geran-2 produits sur le seul mois de juillet, confirmant le dépassement de l'objectif contractuel initial de 6 000 unités/an.2 Cette montée en puissance repose sur une localisation quasi-totale de la production selon Timur Shagivaleev, PDG de la ZES, « 90% des étapes sont désormais réalisées sur site : les barres d'aluminium entrent, les moteurs sortent ; les puces électroniques deviennent de la microélectronique ; le carbone et la fibre de verre deviennent des fuselages ».3 Malgré cette déclaration et une volonté de production en totale autonomie, les services ukrainiens ont pu démontrer que la plupart des composants étrangers dans les drones russes proviennent des États-Unis, tandis que 137 articles sont d'origine allemande. 1 https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_HYN-wow8zg 2 https://globalinitiative.net/wp-content/uploads/2025/05/Qui-fabrique-les-drones-russes-_-Les-migrantes- exploite%CC%81es-pour-le%CC%81conomie-de-guerre-de-la-Russie-GI-TOC-Mai-2025.pdf 3 https://globalinitiative.net/wp-content/uploads/2025/05/Qui-fabrique-les-drones-russes-_-Les-migrantes- exploite%CC%81es-pour-le%CC%81conomie-de-guerre-de-la-Russie-GI-TOC-Mai-2025.pdf AVRIL 2026 7 Planche photo montrant la présence de puces électroniques provenant de Texas Instruments dans les composants identifiés du Geran-5. Source Les deux principaux composants stratégiques de la production résident dans les moteurs et l'électronique, sans lesquels une production à grande échelle du Geran-2 serait impossible. Pour y remédier, la Russie a mis en place de nombreuses entreprises et intermédiaires afin de rationaliser les achats et les livraisons à l'échelle industrielle en provenance de Chine. La Chine joue un rôle décisif dans ce programme, fournissant des composants critiques malgré sa neutralité officielle. AVRIL 2026 8 Graphique des relations entre entreprises russes et chinoises pour Alabuga. Source Un véritable pont aérien a été mis en place depuis l’aéroport d’Urumqi4 en Chine et l’aéroport de Naberezhnye Chelny Begishevo, près du complexe d’Alabuga, avec une escale à l’aéroport de Yekaterinburg Drake LLC et Morgan LLC (basés ZES Alabuga) importent 5,8 M USD (données de 2024) de biens chinois (fibres carbone, pièces moteurs, batteries, électronique) étiquetés parfois « opération militaire spéciale ». 4 Urumqi est un nœud ferroviaire d’importance entre la Chine et la Russie. AVRIL 2026 9 Vol d’un IL-76 d’Aviacon Zitotrans venant de Urumqi vers Naberezhnye Chelny Begishevo. Source : FlightRadar La ville d’ Urumqi est aussi connue pour accueillir les usines produisant les drones et composants des drones chinois Wing Loong, de fabrication chinoise. Il est également à noter que les vols cargo de la même compagnie aérienne effectuent des liaisons entre Urumqi et Taraz, au Kazakhstan, où l'armée de l'air kazakhe a déployé ses drones Wing Loong. Par ailleurs, Juhang Aviation Technology, une entreprise chinoise fournissant des services logistiques à l'industrie de défense russe, a publié en 2025 une offre d'emploi précisant qu'elle recherchait des russophones pour certains clients.5 L’entreprise a publié en 2025 une offre d’emploi précisant rechercher des locuteurs russophones pour certains clients. 5 Cette entreprise chinoise, sous sanctions, est accusée d'avoir fourni à la Russie des hélices de drones, des brouilleurs de signaux, des capteurs et des moteurs. Source / Source AVRIL 2026 10 Pour mieux comprendre l’importance de la Chine dans ce programme, il est nécessaire de se rappeler qu’entre septembre 2023 et juin 2024, au moins 34 entreprises chinoises ont signé des contrats pour un total de plus de 96 millions USD de livraisons vers Alabuga. Ce que Moscou qualifie de production locale à 100 % n'est, en réalité, qu'un simple assemblage de pièces chinoises. Le rôle de l’Iran dans le développement d’Alabuga Les gouvernements russe et iranien ont tous deux joué un rôle clé dans l'établissement de l'usine de drones à Alabuga. Les deux gouvernements ont travaillé à l’organisation d’un échange de connaissances par le biais de visites et de collaboration entre des entreprises clés. Le financement initial était la contribution cruciale du gouvernement russe. Moscou aurait payé à Téhéran 1,7 milliard USD (correspondant à 2 T 07 d’or), en partie sous forme de lingots d'or. Selon l'Union européenne, l'accord concernait un financement par le groupe bancaire russe VTB appartenant à l'État. Les transferts financiers entre la Russie et l’Iran passent par les Emirats Arabes Unis, via la société Generation Trading FZE, basée aux EAU, est identifiée comme AVRIL 2026 11 société-écran du Ministère iranien de la Défense servant de relais pour les flux financiers et logistiques entre Alabuga et l'Iran. Le recours aux EAU comme hub intermédiaire permet de bénéficier de la relative permissivité des contrôles financiers émiratis dans le contexte post-sanctions. L’Iran a apporté son expertise technique. En janvier 2023, Téhéran aurait envoyé une délégation de haut niveau à la ZES dirigée par Abdollah Mehrabi, chef de l'Organisation de recherche et de djihad sur l'autosuffisance de la Force aérospatiale du Corps des gardiens de la révolution islamique (CGRI), et Ghassem Damavandian, le directeur général de Qods Aviation Industries. Mehrabi est également un actionnaire minoritaire de Oje Parvaz Mado Nafar Company, une société iranienne privée qui fournit des moteurs pour les drones d'attaque à sens unique de la série Shahed. En mars 2023, la SEZ aurait envoyé des employés et des étudiants de l'Université polytechnique d'Alabuga en Iran pour recevoir une formation sur la production de cellules aériennes. Déni plausible et zone grise du commerce international Tous ces composants relèvent de la catégorie des biens à double usage. Pékin maintient actuellement une position neutre dans le conflit ukrainien et n'est pas signataire des principaux cadres occidentaux et internationaux de contrôle des exportations de biens militaires. Depuis février 2022, la Chine et la Russie ont établi un partenariat commercial « sans limites ». La Chine et la Russie exploitent divers outils pour acheminer des marchandises vers la Russie, notamment des ventes directes — qu'elles proviennent d'entreprises chinoises ou qu'il s'agisse de produits occidentaux fabriqués dans des usines chinoises — ainsi que le transbordement de produits occidentaux via la Chine. Les entreprises chinoises impliquées commercent légalement par l'intermédiaire d'intermédiaires russes enregistrés dans la Zone économique spéciale d'Alabuga, ce qui rend la traçabilité complexe pour les régimes de sanctions occidentaux. AVRIL 2026 12 Une militarisation du complexe L’Ukraine a cherché plusieurs fois à frapper le complexe industriel avec des avions légers dronisés pour s’affranchir de la distance de plus de 1000 km entre l’Ukraine et Alabuga. Infographie présentant l’avion Aeropark A-22 'Foxbat' dronisé utilisé comme munition téléopérée longue distance. Source Une première attaque le 02 avril 2024 avait endommagé les bâtiments servant de logements des travailleurs, sans toucher les chaînes de production. Dégâts sur un des bâtiments servant à loger les travailleurs après l’attaque ukrainienne. Source Le 23 avril 2025, une nouvelle attaque avec 6 drones avait démontré l'efficacité du système de défense combinant dispositifs de guerre électronique et systèmes anti aériens, permettant d’abattre les 6 drones hors du site. AVRIL 2026 13 Des dispositifs de détection radar sont ainsi observables au Nord du complexe, utilisés pour suivre les activités aériennes dans la zone du complexe industriel qui dispose également d’un héliport. Elles sont complétées par la mise en place d’un brouillage du signal GPS, notamment durant les saisons printemps et été, lorsque la visibilité et la portance aéronautique sont les plus favorables aux attaques. AVRIL 2026 14 Au Sud du complexe a été édifiée une tour d’observation courant 2024 ou début 2025, sur laquelle est installée une tente modulaire en haut de la plateforme. Sur l’image issue de Yandex, il est possible d’observer au moins deux militaires sur cette tour. Déjà en 2023, des mesures de protection étaient mises en place pour à la fois participer à la défense passive du site mais aussi gagner en discrétion depuis l’extérieur du site. AVRIL 2026 15 Au Nord-Ouest du site, à l’extrémité avant la clôture, est présente une emprise permettant d’installer un véhicule de défense anti aérienne sur un monticule, permettant d’être plus haut que la clôture de protection. Ce site, sur une image satellite d’octobre 2025, laisse apercevoir un système PANTSIR, logiquement installé face à l’axe d’arrivée des précédentes attaques de drones ukrainiens. AVRIL 2026 16 Un peu plus au Nord de ce site dédié, un pas de tir aux armes légères d’infanterie est installé, avec des tentes militaires. AVRIL 2026 17 A l’Ouest, une autre plateforme de défense anti-aérienne est présente, avec la même configuration. Sur la plateforme de tir, il est possible de distinguer un véhicule et possiblement une cage anti-drone. AVRIL 2026 18 AVRIL 2026 19 Au Sud du site principal, aux abords immédiats des ateliers de production, une tour d’observation similaire à celle déjà vue aux abords immédiats de la ville. Elle dispose elle aussi d’une tente à son sommet, pouvant abriter des moyens de défense anti- aériens plus légers de type missiles ou des canons bitudes de type ZU-23. AVRIL 2026 20 A l’extérieur du complexe est installé un autre site de défense anti-aérienne. Celui-ci est en bordure immédiate de la route d’accès au site industriel. Sur ce site est installé sur une plateforme de tir embossée un véhicule de défense anti-aérienne protégé par une cage anti-drone. AVRIL 2026 21 A 30km au Sud-Ouest du complexe est installé un radar VHF de contre-batterie de type NEBO, et dans un autre embossement un véhicule militaire possiblement de défense aérienne. Source: Google Earth AVRIL 2026 23 Cette pratique de mise en place de systèmes de défense aérienne se généralise en Russie autour de tous les sites stratégiques, avec des déploiements plus ou moins conséquents. Carte des systèmes de défense aérienne autour du site d’Alabuga - Source : INPACT Les recrutements à Alabuga, entre influence en ligne et éducation patriotique Recrutement de travailleurs non-russes Présenté comme une opportunité de relocalisation professionnelle en Russie, il dissimule la nature militaro-industrielle des activités proposées. Opérant sur des supports divers comme des sites web dédiés, sur Telegram, les plateformes de réseaux sociaux et même des applications de rencontres. Les recrutements AVRIL 2026 24 concernent principalement des jeunes femmes venant d’Afrique, d’Asie centrale ou d’Amérique Latine. Canaux numériques identifiés : ● Site web dédié : startworld.alabuga.ru - présente les postes disponibles (conducteurs d'engins, opérateurs de production, logistique, restauration, carreleurs), les conditions (billet d'avion gratuit, hébergement et restauration inclus, salaire annoncé ~500 USD/mois) et le processus d'intégration étape par étape. ● Telegram : Canal « Alabuga Start Programme » (24 817 abonnés recensés), avec bot de recrutement dédié (@Alabuga_Start_bot). ● Réseaux sociaux : Avant les suppressions d'octobre 2024, les comptes Alabuga (Start, Polytech) totalisaient plus de 53 000 abonnés sur YouTube, Facebook, Instagram et TikTok. Un compte Instagram « work.in_russia » (créé en novembre 2024) est identifié comme page de recrutement global, utilisant des contenus générés par IA et des références culturelles populaires (Harry Potter, dessins animés) pour cibler un public jeune. Une application existe également sur les différents stores. AVRIL 2026 25 ● Applications de rencontre : L'utilisation des applications de dating Tinder et Badoo ont servi à attirer des étudiants africains vers Alabuga Polytech alors que ceux-ci étaient peu engagés dans le processus de recrutement. ● Sites de recherche d’emploi : Sur certains sites recherche d’emploi, des postes vacants dans la ZES d'Alabuga sont proposés. À ce jour, plus de trois mille postes vacants ont été ouverts en Alabuga dans 70 pays différents. Les contenus servant au recrutement sont similaires, montrant des jeunes femmes africaines, asiatiques ou latines souriantes, dans des emplois et rôles positifs, s’accompagnant de séquences d'images touristiques filmées au Tatarstan et durant des activités sportives. Il n’est jamais fait mention des activités militaires du complexe industriel. AVRIL 2026 26 En plus de ces vidéos, il est observé l’emploi de vidéos créées grâce à l’intelligence artificielle. Celles-ci sont majoritairement employées pour diffuser du narratif ciblé. Il n’est jamais fait mention que ces vidéos ne montrent pas des employées réelles d’Alabuga. Vidéo de promotion d’Alabuga diffusée sur la page X du programme Alabuga. Source Analyse de la vidéo montrant qu’elle est générée par une IA. Source AVRIL 2026 27 Ces recrutements en ligne s’accompagnent parfois de recrutement physique dans les pays d’origine. Les autorités de certains pays d’Afrique ont signé des accords spécifiques pour fournir de la main-d'œuvre à la Russie. Pour certains pays comme l’Ethiopie déjà très impliqués dans la fourniture de main d’œuvre bon marché dans les pays du Golfe, ce système permet de limiter l’impact d’une situation économique difficile grâce aux revenus renvoyés vers le pays d’origine. Cas particuliers de la Corée du Nord Les autorités nord-coréennes envisagent l'envoi de 7 000 à 25 000 travailleurs à Alabuga. Le BRICS platform permet à la Russie d'accéder à un vaste vivier de main- d'œuvre potentielle. En 2025, le réseau de recrutement initial ciblait 44 pays (début d'année), étendu à 77 pays à mi-année. L'arrivée de travailleurs nord-coréens aurait un double effet : alimenter la main-d'œuvre russe et transférer des compétences en fabrication de drones OWA vers Pyongyang. AVRIL 2026 28 Recrutement de travailleurs russes « Alabuga Polytech » n'est pas comme n'importe quel autre collège en Russie. La promotion de cet établissement d’élite commence avant même les inscriptions, avec comme examens d'entrée un jeu dans une application sur téléphone, et la mise en avant de conditions de logement de qualité supérieur par la mise en avant d’un studio confortable pour deux pour chaque étudiant. L’idée de départ était de construire une élite technique parmi ces étudiants, aptes à évoluer dans les nouvelles technologies avec aisance et leadership. Cette promotion était aussi faite en ligne pour attirer des talents de l’étranger. Pour les jeunes russes, la perspective d’intégrer « Alabuga Polytech »est une opportunité majeure comparée au reste du système scolaire russe. Dans une vidéo de communication en langue russe dont le but est de recruter de nouveaux étudiants, étaient présents de jeunes hommes et femmes ayant une apparence physique laissant peu de doute sur leur état de mineurs. Derrière un message patriotique, c’est bien la promotion du travail des enfants qui est faite, en violation des traités internationaux sur le sujet. En réalité, les étudiants qui y étudient évoluent au sein d’un double programme, géré par la direction d'Alabuga. Dans d’autres vidéos, les étudiants décrivent les conditions de travail et les salaires, affirmant que les étudiants de deuxième année peuvent gagner, à savoir 150.000 roubles par mois (1 900 USD) et les étudiants de troisième année 350.000 roubles par mois (4,500 USD) - nettement plus élevé que le salaire mensuel médian de 87.000 roubles (1,100 USD) dans la région. Une étudiante de première année affirme que pour sa seconde année, elle commencera à gagner de l'argent en assemblant des drones, ajoutant que ses parents sont fiers d'elle. Dans une autre vidéo, Alexander, étudiant de deuxième année, affirme qu’il gagne déjà 150.000 roubles en travaillant comme spécialiste de l’inspection dans la « plus grande usine de production de drones au monde ». Un jeune homme dans une autre vidéo raconte qu’après avoir commencé à travailler dans la production de drones, son père a commencé à l’appeler « vrai homme ». AVRIL 2026 29 Au-delà d’un accès à des études de haut niveau technique, c’est bien l’éducation patriotique qui est au centre du système de gestion de « Alabuga Polytech ». La réalité d’une éducation à la dure, avec des punitions collectives en cas d’écarts. Les étudiants d'Alabuga Polytech 2 creusent des tranchées comme punition. Source: v1.ru Les conditions de travail sur les différentes chaînes d’assemblage, que ce soit pour les travailleurs étrangers ou les étudiants-travailleurs russes laissent montrer des journées de travail supérieures à 12h avec des heures supplémentaires non payées, la confiscation des passeports pour les travailleurs étrangers avec des difficultés à pouvoir démissionner, un encadrement strict à l’intérieur du complexe industriel déclaré zone sensible. AVRIL 2026 30 Typologies de recrutement En suivant les différentes campagnes de recrutement organisées autour du programme Alabuga, il est possible d’identifier les typologies de recrutement suivantes et les risques associés de travail forcé : Profil Origine Canal principal Rôle réel probable Statut légal Étudiants russes (dont mineurs) Tatarstan et régions voisines Alabuga Polytech, filière duale Assemblage Shahed/Geran sur lignes de prod. Travailleurs nord-coréens (prévisionnels) Corée du Nord Accord gouvernemental RPDC-Russie Assemblage drones (7 000 à 25 000 estimés) Accord d'État non confirmé officiellement - fort risque de travail forcé AVRIL 2026 31 Quelles perspectives pour Alabuga En conservant la production actuelle, Alabuga pourrait produire entre 60 000 et 80 000 drones par an, en combinaison avec le site secondaire d'Izhevsk. Ce dernier site, moins connu qu’Alabuga, revêt toutefois une importance similaire car il y est produit les drones Garpiya-3, avec le même pont aérien depuis Urumqi. Capture d’écran du site internet de Izhevsk Aircraft Plane. Source Vue de l’usine de Kupol à Izhevsk, au Tatarstan, produisant les drones Garpiya-3. Source Pour pouvoir maintenir de telles cadences, il faudra une main d’œuvre plus importante. Avec l'achèvement des 4 sites de logement, la capacité d’accueillir pourra voir l’accueil de 71 000 travailleurs, dont potentiellement 7 000 à 25 000 Nord- Coréens. Après avoir développé son propre outil de production, la Russie est désormais dans une phase d’autonomie technologique. La localisation à 90% est atteinte depuis fin AVRIL 2026 32 2025, rendant les frappes sur la supply chain iranienne ou les sanctions sur les composants iraniens largement obsolètes comme levier de pression. La Russie est potentiellement exportatrice des évolutions du Geran avec les modèles Geran-3 et Geran-5, notamment vers ses partenaires dont l’Iran, dans un contexte de reprise des hostilités au Moyen-Orient. Pour perturber les unités de production, l'efficacité opérationnelle des frappes ukrainiennes suggère que des frappes précises sur les hubs logistiques restent le levier perturbateur le plus efficace à court terme.

1fzwxJjsRO_t_eR-Nte2UY4N0P1DCLOzW.text: APRIL 2026 1 Alabuga, the central hub of the Russian hybrid threat Sanction Circumvention, Increased Production, Expanded and Militarised Facilities and More Foreign Labour APRIL 2026 2 Executive summary The conflict in Ukraine has been the first major war to see the widespread use of one-way attack drones with Russia’s Geran-2, evolved from the Iranian Shahed-136, used to target Ukrainian civilian and military infrastructure on an almost daily basis. The last quarter of 2024 saw more drone attacks than the previous 23 months combined – a trend that continued in 2025, climbing to over 6,000 a month by the second half of the year. This dramatic increase has been made possible through the significant expansion of production facilities at the Alabuga Special Economic Zone (SEZ) in Tatarstan, Russia. Alabuga underwent major development in late 2024 through 2025: expanded drone production facilities, new accommodation space for a larger workforce, as well as the increasingly fortification of the site with air defences and other security measures. With the completion of four housing complexes, Alabuga has developed a capacity to accommodate an estimated 71,000 workers, which would represent a significant uplift in staff and production capacity. Working alongside the Russian state, Alabuga SEZ has sought to attract patriotic Russians to work at the facilities. More controversial, however, has been the concerted effort to recruit individuals from overseas through the Alabuga Start campaign. Presented as a professional relocation opportunity in Russia, Alabuga’s slick marketing materials conceal the military-industrial nature of the activities offered to recruits – primarily young women from the Global South. These international recruits have also been supplemented by state-to-state agreements to supply workers from historic allies, including between allegedly 7,000 and 25,000 North Koreans, raising concerns over potential forced labour. While states such as North Korea may be willing conspirators in this military machine – and Russians participate out of patriotic duty – international recruits may be unaware they are being drawn into the heart of the Kremlin’s new military-industrial complex. This report examines the development, operations and recruitment at Alabuga, which represents a case study of the military, technological and human resource collaboration between Russia, Iran, China and North Korea that has enabled the war in Ukraine and poses risks far beyond. APRIL 2026 3 An industrial architecture in full expansion The Alabuga complex is located in the Special Economic Zone of the Republic of Tatarstan, in the immediate vicinity of the city of Alabuga. It is a closed "industrial campus", placed under the joint governance of JSC Alabuga SEZ, the authorities of Tatarstan and Moscow, with strong involvement from the Russian Ministry of Defense. Two main buildings house the drone production facilities: ● Building 8.1 (East): Production partially dedicated to Geran-2 drones, the rest used for other industrial projects. ● Building 8.2 (West): Entirely dedicated to the assembly of the Geran-2. Analysis of satellite images from September 2023 reveals less snow accumulation on the roofs of these two buildings compared to neighboring structures, a sign of continuous industrial activity and significant heating, confirming their intensive use. Overlay of satellite view and internal floor plan for drone production. Source APRIL 2026 4 Since early 2025, there has been ongoing construction at the complex to expand the production facilities and provide housing for workers. Satellite imagery showing the expansion of the drone production complex in Albuga and housing areas designated for foreign workers. Source: Sentinel-2 A closer look at the new housing developments. Source: t.me/DniproOfficial Between the end of 2024 and July 2025, at least eight new warehouse/workshop type structures were constructed near drone production buildings, visible on satellite imagery. 240 new prefabricated modular residential buildings were under construction during this period, with an estimated capacity to accommodate 41,000 workers. With an estimated 25,000 people working at the complex in 2025, this would represent a significant uplift in staffing numbers and production capacity. APRIL 2026 5 In early July 2025, a temporary tent city of 119 tents (Camp "Alabuga Build”) was set up in a few weeks near the production lines, housing 2,500 students mobilised by the “Russian Student Brigades”.1 Satellite view of the Geran-2 drone production complex in Alabuga on February 6, 2026. Image source: VANDOR; Analysis: INPACT On February 6, 2026, it is possible to observe a new production unit and five housing areas for drone assembly workers. To better support production activities, a new transport hub has also been built, providing direct rail links between Russia and China, designed to transport up to 100,000 containers per year. This complements regular flights from Chinese factories to Tatarstan. The ramp-up of production 1 https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=6VqH1z2GbEg APRIL 2026 6 The expanded facilities at Alabuga have enabled Russia to quickly ramp up production, while also modernising the original Shahed-136 design, copied from Iran. According to leaked data2, the Russians have increased the drone's warhead from 54kg to 92kg, replacing the standard charges with thermobaric and incendiary charges. Thanks to noise-canceling antennas, the drone can bypass Ukrainian jamming systems, and the latest modifications include high-definition video cameras and 4G modems to correct flight paths using SIM cards from Ukrainian or Polish GSM operators, among others. These drones cost between $30,000 and $50,000 USD to produce. With the increased manufacturing capacity, production rates have seen a dramatic acceleration: ● End of 2023: 20 Geran-2 drones produced per day (2 teams), i.e. ~600/month. ● April 2024: 4,500 units delivered to the Ministry of Defence. ● May 2025: a rate of 2,700 Geran-2 and 2,500 Gerbera (decoy drones) per month, i.e. more than 5,000 devices per month.3 ● July 2025: 6,100 Geran-2 produced in July alone, exceeding the initial contractual target of 6,000 units/year.4 Timur Shagivaleev, CEO of Alabuga SEZ, claims the increase in capacity has been driven by an almost total localisation of production, saying "90% of the steps are now carried out on site: aluminum bars go in, engines come out; electronic chips become microelectronics; carbon and fiberglass become fuselages."5 Despite this assertion and desire for completely autonomous production, Ukrainian government research has demonstrated that the drones are still reliant on foreign components, with most coming China, but also Germany, Japan and Switzerland, and other European countries allied to Ukraine6. 2 https://en.defence- ua.com/weapon_and_tech/shahed_136s_new_90_kg_warhead_and_other_findings_of_the_alabuga_data_lea k-10265.html 3https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_HYN-wow8zg 4https://globalinitiative.net/wp-content/uploads/2025/05/Who-makes-Russian-drones-_-Migrant-women- exploited-for-Russia's-war-economy-GI-TOC-May-2025.pdf 5https://globalinitiative.net/wp-content/uploads/2025/05/Who-makes-Russian-drones-_-Migrant-women- exploited-for-Russia's-war-economy-GI-TOC-May-2025.pdf 6 https://www.dw.com/uk/rosijski-droni-v-ukraini-ak-u-nih-opinilisa-nimecki-zapcastini/a-75951532 APRIL 2026 7 European components found in the Geran-2 drone by HUR, Ukraine’s military intelligence (Source: OCCRP) Photographic plate showing the presence of electronic chips from Texas Instruments in the identified components of the Geran-5 (Source: Ukrainian GUR) The two main strategic components of production are the engines and the electronics, without which large-scale production of the Geran-2 would be impossible. Russia has established numerous companies and intermediaries to streamline industrial-scale APRIL 2026 8 procurement and deliveries of critical components from China, which plays a crucial role in the drone program despite its official neutrality. Relations between Russian and Chinese companies in Alabuga (Source: Frontelligence Insight) An air bridge has been established from Urumqi Airport in China7 and Naberezhnye Airport Chelny Begishevo, near the Alabuga complex, with a stopover at Yekaterinburg airport. Drake LLC and Morgan LLC (based in Alabuga SEZ) import USD 5.8m (2024 data) of Chinese goods (carbon fibers, engine parts, batteries, electronics), sometimes labeled with "special military operation". 7Urumqi is an important railway hub between China and Russia. APRIL 2026 9 Flight of an Aviacon IL-76 Zitotrans coming from Urumqi to Naberezhnye Chelny Begishevo (Source: FlightRadar) The city of Urumqi is also known for hosting factories producing drones and components for China’s Wing Loong drones. It is also notable that journeys made by cargo planes of the same airline show trips between Urumqi and Taraz in Kazakhstan, where Kazakhstan’s Air Force bases its Wing Loong drones. Moreover, Juhang Aviation Technology, one Chinese company involved in the logistics required by the Russian defense industry, published a job offer in 2025 specifying that it was looking for Russian speakers for certain clients.8 8This Chinese company, under sanctions, is accused of providing Russia with drone propellers, signal jammers, sensors and engines. Source / Source APRIL 2026 10 Job advert posted by Juhang Aviation Technology (Shenzhen) seeking Russian speakers The scale and importance of China’s involvement in the drone program cannot be underestimated – between September 2023 and June 2024, at least 34 Chinese companies signed contracts worth more than USD 96 million for deliveries to Alabuga. What Moscow likes to frame as 100% local production is, in reality, an assembly line for largely Chinese parts. Importantly, all of the components supplied by China fall under the category of dual- use goods. Beijing officially maintains a neutral stance in the Ukrainian conflict and is not a signatory to the main export control measures for military equipment. The Chinese companies involved trade legally through Russian intermediaries, registered in the Alabuga Special Economic Zone, making traceability difficult for Western sanctions regimes. APRIL 2026 11 The role of Iran in the development of Alabuga The Russian and Iranian governments both played a key role in establishing the drone factory in Alabuga, organising knowledge exchange through visits and collaborations between key companies. The initial funding was the crucial contribution of the Russian government. Moscow reportedly paid Tehran $1.7 billion (equivalent to 2.07 tons of gold), partly in the form of gold bars. According to the European Union, the agreement involved by the Russian state-owned banking group VTB. Financial transfers between Russia and Iran are routed through the United Arab Emirates, via the UAE-based company Generation Trading FEZ, identified as a front company for the Iranian Ministry of Defense, serving as a conduit for financial and logistical flows between Alabuga and Iran. Using the UAE as an intermediary hub allows Iran to benefit from the relatively permissive Emirati financial controls in the post-sanctions context. Iran contributed its technical expertise. In January 2023, Tehran reportedly sent a high-level delegation to the SEZ headed by Abdullah Mehrabi, head of the Research and Jihad Organization on Self-Reliance of the Aerospace Force of the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC), and Ghassem Damavandian, the Managing Director of Qods Aviation Industries. Mehrabi is also a minority shareholder in Oje Parvaz Mado Nafar Company, a private Iranian company that supplies engines for the Shahed series of one-way attack drones. In March 2023, Alabuga SEZ reportedly sent employees and students from Alabuga Polytechnic University to Iran to receive training on the production of . An intelligence assessment leaked to The Washington Post suggested more than 200 employees and 100 students were trained in Iran in spring 2023. Plausible denial and the grey area of international trade All of these components fall into the category of dual-use goods. Beijing currently maintains a neutral stance in the Ukrainian conflict and is not a signatory to the main Western and International export control measures for military goods. Since February 2022, China and Russia established a “no limits” trading partnership. China and Russia leverage various tools to funnel items to Russia, including direct sales either from Chinese companies or of Western products made in Chinese factories and transshipment of Western products through China. The Chinese companies involved trade legally through Russian intermediaries registered in the Alabuga Special Economic Zone, making traceability complex for Western sanctions regimes. APRIL 2026 12 The militarisation of the complex Ukraine has repeatedly sought to strike the industrial complex with drone-equipped light aircraft, overcoming the distance of more than 1000km between Ukraine and Alabuga. Infographic showing the Aeropark A-22 'Foxbat' drone used as a long-range remotely operated munition. Source: Army Recognition A first attack on April 2, 2024, damaged the buildings used as workers' housing, without affecting the production lines. Damage to one of the buildings used to house workers after the Ukrainian attack. Source: RFERL APRIL 2026 13 With Ukraine having shown it was capable of striking Alabuga and its success in hitting sensitive sites with launches deep inside Russia, most notably in Operation Spiderweb in June 2025, Russia has increased protection at the Alabuga site. On April 23, 2025, an attempted Ukrainian attack with six drones failed, demonstrating the effectiveness of a defense system that combined electronic warfare devices and anti-air systems. All six drones were shot down before reaching the site. Radar detection devices can be observed to the north of the complex, which monitor aerial activities in the area of the industrial complex, which also has a heliport. Two radar antennae visible on street view images (Source: Yandex) This is complemented by the implementation of GPS signal jamming, particularly during the spring and summer seasons, when visibility and flight conditions are most favorable to attacks. APRIL 2026 14 Maps of GPS jamming, showing high interference in July 2025 (top), when weather conditions are more favourable for drone incursions, and March 2026 (bottom). Source: GPSJam.org To the south of the complex, an observation tower was built in 2024 or early 2025, topped with a modular tent. The image from Yandex shows at least two soldiers on this tower. APRIL 2026 15 Street view images showing a watch tower manned by soldiers at Alabuga. Source: Yandex Already in 2023, protective measures were put in place to provide passive defense and increase privacy and security at the site, including a new security perimeter, checkpoint and corridor connecting buildings. Increased security features at the Alabuga site in 2023. Source: Maxar More recent satellite imagery shows the expansion of military equipment and facilities at Alabuga. To the northwest of the site, at the far end before the perimeter fence, APRIL 2026 16 there is a platform allowing for the installation of an anti-aircraft defense vehicle on a mound, providing elevation above the protective fence. A satellite image from October 2025 shows a vehicle at the position, possibly a Pantsir system, logically positioned to face the direction of previous Ukrainian drone attacks. Satellite imagery from October 2025 showing an air defence system deployed at the Alabuga site. Source: VANDOR A little further north of the dedicated air defence site, a small arms infantry firing range is set up, with military tents. APRIL 2026 17 Satellite imagery from October 2025 showing a shooting range and military tents at the Alabuga site. Source: VANDOR To the west, another air defense platform is present, with the same configuration. On the firing platform, a vehicle and possibly an anti-drone cage can be distinguished. APRIL 2026 18 Satellite imagery from October 2025 showing a second air defence system deployed at the Alabuga site. Source: VANDOR South of the main site, in the immediate vicinity of the production workshops, there is another observation tower. It too has a tent at the top, capable of housing lighter anti- aircraft defense systems, such as missiles or ZU-23 type cannons. APRIL 2026 19 Satellite imagery from October 2025 showing a watchtower and protection tunnel. Source: VANDOR APRIL 2026 20 Outside the complex is another air defence site, located immediately adjacent to the access road to the industrial site. Satellite imagery shows an air defense vehicle protected by an anti-drone cage mounted on a recessed firing platform. Satellite imagery from October 2025 showing another air defence site and watchtowers. Source: VANDOR Further away from Alabuga, there is also evidence of increased defences. 30km southwest, satellite imagery shows a NEBO type VHF counter-battery radar and a military vehicle, possibly for air defence. APRIL 2026 21 APRIL 2026 22 Satellite images showing a counter-battery radar and defensive position. Source: Google Earth The increase in defence measures on the Alabuga site and in the surrounding area underscore the importance of the complex to the Russian war effort, as well as a growing concern over Ukrainian capabilities to strike the facilities. Recruitment in Alabuga: Between online influence and Patriotic Education Recruitment of non-Russian workers Presented as a professional relocation opportunity in Russia, Alabuga’s slick marketing materials conceal the military-industrial nature of the activities offered. Recruitment primarily targets young women from Africa, Central Asia, and Latin America, with a dedicated website and content on various platforms, including Telegram, Facebook, Instagram, and even dating apps. Identified digital channels: ● Dedicated website: startworld.alabuga.ru – presents the available positions (machine operators, production operators, logistics, catering, tilers), the APRIL 2026 23 conditions (free airfare, accommodation and meals included, advertised salary ~500 USD/month) and the step-by-step integration process. ● Telegram: Channel “Alabuga Start Programme” (24,817 subscribers recorded), with dedicated recruitment bot (@Alabuga_Start_bot ). ● Social media: Before accounts were deleted in October 2024, the Alabuga (Start, Polytech) accounts had over 53,000 followers across YouTube, Facebook, Instagram, and TikTok. An Instagram account, "work.in_russia " (created in November 2024), is identified as a global recruitment page, using AI-generated content and popular cultural references (Harry Potter, cartoons) to target a young audience. An app is also available on various app stores. APRIL 2026 24 ● Dating apps: The use of dating apps Tinder and Badoo has been used to attract African students to Alabuga Polytech and increase engagement in the recruitment process. ● Job search websites: Some job search websites advertise vacancies in the Alabuga Special Economic Zone. To date, more than three thousand vacancies have been opened in Alabuga in 70 different countries. The recruitment materials maintain a consistent visual narrative, showing smiling young African, Asian, or Latin American women in positive jobs and roles, accompanied by tourist footage filmed in Tatarstan and during sporting activities. There is never any mention of the industrial complex's military activities. APRIL 2026 25 In addition to videos of real recruits, Alabuga marketing content also uses AI- generated content to disseminate targeted narratives. It is not acknowledged that the videos do not depict real Alabuga employees – or real people. Promotional video for Alabuga shown on page X of the Alabuga program. Source: Alabuga X account Analysis of the video shows that it was generated by AI. Source: Image Whisperer APRIL 2026 26 These online recruitment efforts are sometimes accompanied by physical recruitment in the countries of origin. Alabuga has signed official partnerships and MoUs with local organisations, such as bilateral friendship associations, to act as exclusive partners and handle recruitment, hosting marketing events and processing applicants. These recruitment efforts, largely focused on young women in Africa, Asia and Latin America, targeted 44 countries in 2024, expanding to 77 countries in 2025. State-to-state recruitment efforts Given the significant expansion of accommodation at Alabuga – providing for approximately 42,000 additional workers – it is unlikely this capacity will be filled by the relatively slow and ‘organic’ recruitment process of online and in-country marketing. A more significant and rapid source of recruits is likely to be inter-governmental bilateral agreements, with countries agreeing to supply labour to Alabuga SEZ. In March 2025, Uzbekistan, which has long been a source of low-cost manual labour to the Russian market, signed an agreement with Alabuga to ‘attract’ 10,000 additional Uzbek workers to the facility. Ethiopia, already heavily involved in providing cheap labour to the Gulf states, also signed an agreement in August 2025, although the scale of the deal is not clear. APRIL 2026 27 The signing of an agreement between Ethiopia and Alabuga to supply labour. Source: X There have also been persistent rumours over the role of North Korean workers. North Korea has been actively involved in Russia’s war in Ukraine since late 2024, officially confirming they had sent troops in April 2025, estimated to number between 10,000-15,000. In mid-2025, Japanese outlet NHK, citing unnamed Western diplomatic sources, suggested North Korea was considering sending up to 25,000 workers to Alabuga, which had the potential to increase drone production by up to 33%. In November, Ukraine’s GRU stated that Russia planned to bring in 12,000 workers from North Korea by the end of 2025, reporting that meetings had taken place between Russian officials and representatives of Jihyang Technology Trade Company, a North Korean firm responsible for selecting and recruiting the workers. While current numbers are unclear, the arrival of North Korean workers would have a dual effect: bolstering the Russian workforce and transferring expertise in OWA drone manufacturing to Pyongyang. Recruitment of Russian workers – ‘the officer class’ In addition to overseas recruitment of workers, Alabuga has also made a considerable effort to attract young Russians. Alabuga Polytech is framed as a patriotic opportunity to acquire technical skills and experience, while offering attractive superior living conditions and benefits, such as a comfortable studio apartment for two students. A Russian-language promotional video aimed at potential recruits featured notably young men and women working in the drone factory. Behind a patriotic message, the focus is very much on work within the factory, rather than any academic offer or progression. In reality, the students are effectively enrolled in an employment programme managed by the Alabuga administration. APRIL 2026 28 In other videos, students describe working conditions and salaries, claiming that second-year students can earn 150,000 rubles per month (US$1,900) and third-year students 350,000 rubles per month (US$4,500) – significantly higher than the median monthly salary of 87,000 rubles (US$1,100) in the region. One first-year student says that in her second year, she will start earning money assembling drones, adding that her parents are proud of her. In another, Alexander, a second-year student, claims that he already earns 150,000 rubles working as an inspection specialist at the “world’s largest drone production factory.” A young man in another video recounts that after he started working in drone production, his father began calling him a “real man”. Patriotic education is at the heart of the "Alabuga Polytech" management system. This translates into a harsh education, with collective punishments for any deviations. Students at Alabama Polytech 2 are digging trenches as punishment. Source: v1.ru With Alabuga’s several strands of recruitment – Russian nationals, overseas recruits from the Global South, and state-to-state agreements, including North Koreans – it is APRIL 2026 29 apparent there is a tiered ‘employment’ structure within Alabuga, with varying degrees of ‘freedom’ and benefits for those working at the site. Nevertheless, the working conditions on assembly lines, whether for foreign workers or Russian student workers, are reportedly harsh – working days exceeding 12 hours with unpaid overtime, confiscation of passports for foreign workers and barriers to leaving employment, and strict supervision within the industrial complex declared a sensitive zone. APRIL 2026 30 Recruitment typologies By following the various recruitment campaigns organized around the Alabuga program, it is possible to identify the following recruitment typologies and associated risks for forced labour: Profile Origin Main Channel Probable actual role Legal status Russian students (including minors) Tatarstan and neighboring regions Albuga Polytech, dual-track program Shahed / Geran assembly on production lines. Not transparent African students (including minors) Sub-Saharan Africa, Central Asia Schools, dating apps, Polytech Workshop workforce, assembly, logistics Not transparent African women (18-25 years old) West and Central Africa Alabuga Start, social media, Telegram Production operators, cleaning, handling Documented risk of forced labor Latin American women Latin America Alabuga Start (dedicated campaigns) Assembly line work, services at the complex Documented risk of forced labor Various migrant workers Worldwide (with BRICS targeted) Alabuga Start World website, Telegram bot Logistics, catering, production Opaque recruitment North Korean workers (projected) North Korea DPRK-Russia Government Agreement Drone assembly (estimated 7,000 to 25,000) State agreement not officially confirmed APRIL 2026 31 The prospects for Alabuga If production levels increase in line with its current rates, Alabuga could produce between 60,000 and 80,000 drones per year, in combination with the secondary site in Izhevsk. This latter site, less well-known than Alabuga, is important as it produces the Garpiya-3 drones, utilising the same air corridor from Urumqi. Screenshot from the Izhevsk Aircraft Plane website. Source View of the Kupol factory in Izhevsk, Tatarstan, producing the Garpiya-3 drones. Source To achieve these higher production rates, a larger workforce will be required. With the completion of the four housing complexes, Alabuga has developed a capacity to accommodate an estimated 71,000 workers – a considerable number – potentially including between 7,000 and 25,000 North Koreans. APRIL 2026 32 By developing its own production facilities, Russia has now reached a stage of technological self-sufficiency. The localisation of 90% of its production has been achieved since the end of 2025, reducing the likelihood of attacks on its supply chain.9 Beyond its key role in the Russian military arsenal against Ukraine, Alabuga's production and technological developments will likely have far-reaching repercussions. Russia is a potential exporter of Geran drone upgrades, such as the Geran-3 and Geran-5 models, particularly to its partners, including Iran and North Korea, which would increase the drone threat in other theaters of operation.10 The significant development at the Alabuga site, its increasing defences, and the scale of required recruitment underscores the importance of the complex to the Russian war effort. As such, it is likely to remain a key target for Ukrainian action. And while states such as North Korea may be willing conspirators in this military machine – and Russians participate out of patriotic duty - international recruits need to understand the reality of ‘opportunities’ at Alabuga – they are being drawn into the new heart of the Russian military-industrial complex. 9 The Iranian supply chain, now obsolete, was also easily targeted by the various sanctions. 10 https://www.washingtonpost.com/world/2026/03/27/russia-iran-drones-shahed-war-israel- ukraine/5b81d8fc-2a08-11f1-a0f2-3ba4c9fe08ac_story.html

1tVGrCRwSiSImbA8Qhi6BhYEp4SAVMqiw.text: T H E B U S I N E S S O F D E S P A I R I N P A C T F E B R U A R Y 2 0 2 6F E B R U A R Y 2 0 2 6 T h e R u s s i a n a r m y ’ s r e c r u i t i n g o f A f r i c a n f i g h t e r s February 2026 1 Contents Executive summary 2 Africa, a human resource for Russia’s war in Ukraine 5 More than a thousand African fighters recruited by Russia since 2023 5 Recruitment typology and profiles chosen 5 A visible, rapid recruitment process offering quick benefits 14 African fighters as cannon fodder 21 The tactics and networks of recruitment: The business of war 22 The network of travel agencies 23 Capitalising on the friends of the Kremlin 37 When recruits become recruiters 39 Fake jobs postings 45 Illegal immigration networks: a goldmine for forced recruitment 48 Disrupting networks, disrupting the war business 50 Understanding the appeal 50 Reactions from the countries concerned 53 Conclusions and Recommendations 54 Appendices 56 February 2026 2 Executive summary Since 2023, the Russian Federation has intensified its efforts to address the shortage of personnel in the Russian armed forces, as it seeks to manage the grinding war of attrition in Ukraine and the strengthening of international sanctions targeting its economic capabilities, the Russian military-industrial sector, and the activities of its paramilitary networks abroad. Faced with these constraints, the Kremlin has launched a structured recruitment campaign, targeting historic allies and the Global South, with a particular focus on the African continent. This campaign aims to recruit and deploy African nationals to the Ukrainian theatre, leveraging transnational networks that exploit persistent socio- economic vulnerabilities. INPACT has identified recruitment tactics that target young people aspiring to pursue higher education abroad, particularly in fields presented as strategic; job seekers eager for economic opportunities; and candidates for irregular migration to Europe, for whom Russia is presented as an alternative route. Working with contacts and the "Khachu Zhit/I want to live" project, INPACT obtained several files containing lists of recruits from Africa, including one more comprehensive file with two tabs:  A list of 1,417 nationals from African countries with their full name, date of birth, military registration number, the date they signed their military contract in Russia, and their citizenship.  A second list detailing those killed in action. It includes the name, date of birth, military service number, contract signing date, recorded date of death, number of months enlisted before death, citizenship, and unit of affiliation within the Russian army. INPACT set about verifying this file by investigating the digital traces, including social media postings, left by the purported recruits. INPACT was able to identify a sufficient number of individuals who had posted about their time in Russia and military involvement to conclude that the list was legitimate. A few errors were identified regarding the nationalities of some individuals, which can likely be attributed to incomplete data entry. Further research also identified combat deaths not mentioned on the list, indicating that the list was not exhaustive and the number of African nationals recruited is likely to be higher. Based on these list and subsequent research, INPACT has concluded that the recruitment of African nationals is not an isolated phenomenon, but rather the core of a deliberate and organised strategy. These recruits have been integrated into assault waves designed to overwhelm Ukrainian defensive lines, contributing to a strategy of February 2026 3 attrition. From the recruits' perspective, recruitment is driven by a desire for social and geographic mobility, within a context marked by structural poverty, political instability, and recurring crises on the African continent. This asymmetrical convergence of interests constitutes the core target of the recruitment campaigns observed. INPACT identified several complementary recruitment tactics, including the use of travel agencies operating as logistical intermediaries, the involvement of local pro- Russian individuals and organisations, and recruitment networks based on co- optation, in which former recruits become recruiters themselves. These schemes rely heavily on bogus job offers, promises of education or administrative regularisation, and irregular immigration channels. INPACT also identified an example where the involvement of Russian security services was explicitly mentioned – the Federal Security Service (FSB), which is believed to coordinate all or part of these recruitment networks. A significant portion of recruitment activities take place online, primarily on social media, which today constitutes one of the main channels for information and migration planning for young Africans. Research and reporting took place from July 2025 to January 2026 Published by INPACT contact@investig-impact.org Thanks to our Ukrainian contacts February 2026 4 Their names are Serge, Nabil, Samuel, and Eric, and they come from different countries on the African continent. What they have in common is they fight as foreign soldiers in the Russian army. They tell us their journeys all started the same way: mysterious travel agencies, extensively advertised on social media, offering heavily discounted airline tickets to Russia. The role of these agencies is to connect young men with the Russian army. We speak to Ahmed [name changed to protect his identity], a young man under thirty, originally from a North African country, serving in the Russian army. The exchange is not easy, and Ahmed makes no secret of his hostility toward European countries. Haunted by the death of a relative – whom he claims was killed by the French – he displays a thirst for revenge and is not shy about making threats. According to him, European countries, like France, are next on Russia's list. He tells us several times that he will come for us. He likens the Russian Army to an international football team, made up of the best players from diverse backgrounds who have taken the nationality of the country they now represent. He tells us that his second nationality is now Russian, that he has obtained a passport, and that he is proud to serve alongside the Russians. Ahmed indicates that he is not alone in this situation, and that many foreigners have made this choice. Serge, Nabil, Samuel, Eric and Ahmed were among almost 1,500 names provided to INPACT on a database of African recruits to the Russian Army. Drawing on this list, INPACT set out to investigate the individuals who have made this journey, seeking to understand their motivations and experience, and the recruitment process which took them from Africa to the frontlines in Ukraine. February 2026 5 Africa, a human resource for Russia’s war in Ukraine In 2022, Yevgeny Prigozhin's Wagner Group scoured Russia’s prisons for recruits1, offering ‘freedom’ in exchange for a spell in the military, with many ending their lives as cannon-fodder on the frontlines in Ukraine. However, with sustained losses from attritional warfare and no end to the fighting in sight, Russia has shifted its efforts to recruiting foreign nationals, utilising historic ties in countries such as Kazakhstan, Tajikistan, Kyrgyzstan and Cuba, and its growing relations with the Global South to target recruits in Nepal, Sri Lanka, Iraq, and across African countries. INPACT, working with contacts and the Ukrainian project "Khochu Zhit” 2 (translated: I want to Live), was able to obtain several lists of nationals from African countries engaged with Russian military, with contracts signed from January 1, 2023 to September 5, 2025. This combined database formed the starting point of our investigation. More than a thousand African fighters recruited by Russia since 2023 The database lists a total of 1,417 African men who have signed a formal contract to enlist and fight in the Russian Army since 2023. Importantly, the database does not appear to be exhaustive, and the total number of African recruits is likely to be higher – INPACT crosschecked the list against the names of African recruits reported killed in Ukraine and discovered new names, not on the database. According to INPACT's analysis, the recruitment of fighters has been steadily increasing since 2023, rising from 177 in the first year to 592 in 2024 (a figure directly confirmed3 by Russia at the time) and 647 in 2025. These figures do not include recruits for the Alabuga industrial zone4, home to the Iranian drone manufacturing plant, which has its own recruitment programme targeting Global South countries, in particular young women. Recruitment typology and profiles chosen Of the 1,417 African recruits identified in the database as having joined the Russian armed forces or associated private military companies, INPACT's analysis shows 1https://www.bbc.com/news/world-europe-62911618 2https://x.com/hochuzhit_com 3 https://topwar.ru/246562-afrika-i-svo-afrikanskij-vektor-rossii.html 4https://www.business-humanrights.org/fr/derni%C3%A8res-actualit%C3%A9s/russia-women-from- africa-recruited-via-alabuga-start-programme-on-social-media-to-work-in-weapons-factories-incl-cos- responses-non-responses/ February 2026 6 significant contingents from Egypt (361 men), Cameroon (335 men) and Ghana (234 men) with a distribution as follows5: The average age among African recruits is 31 years, with a stronger representation of the 18-25 age category (150 recruits). The oldest is 57 years old, an Egyptian national, while the youngest is 18 years old from Ghana. The analysis also showed interesting anomalies among the names in the lists. Some recruits with Central African-sounding names are declared to be of Algerian nationality, while others are listed with Russian-sounding names. Our working hypothesis is that the large Algerian contingent is a result of incomplete data entry: in the absence of a nationality, the value has defaulted to the first African country alphabetically, Algeria. INPACT was also able to identify recruits who were already established and present in Russia – likely through university programs – and individuals who were recruited in Africa and travelled to Russia. Africans captured by Ukrainian forces on the battlefield all give similar accounts, describing a rapid deployment after signing a contract they could not read or understand, because it was written in Cyrillic. A 25-year-old Egyptian man, captured near the village of Peremoha, told his captors28 he was a graduate of a language program in Russia, where he studied English and Russian. He had signed a contract with the Russian forces on March 3, 2025, due to his expiring immigration status in Russia and the validity of his visa. He explained that he was placed in a room resembling a prison cell, then Russian soldiers loaded him into a truck and took him to a training ground, where he was assigned to an assault team sent to fight in the Donbas. The list of African recruits obtained by INPACT shows 316 deaths in combat to date out of the 1,417 individuals on the list, from the following nationalities: 28 https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Mz2YwYEgNU8 February 2026 22 Cameroonian nationals suffered the highest number of deaths (94), followed by Ghanaians (55) and Egyptians (52). In the 18-25 age group, 60 African recruits were killed in action in Ukraine, with the number of listed recruits killed while fighting in the Russian army totalling 316. The duration of service of those killed in action averages only six months, highlighting the fate of many African recruits as cannon fodder for the frontline. Over 50 recruits served only a month before being killed (51 total), while the longest duration listed was 19 months, undertaken by an Egyptian national and another from Benin before their deaths. The recruits killed in action came from 37 different military units. Most of these units were involved in costly assaults in Ukraine, such as the 1008th Motorised Rifle Regiment of the 6th Motorised Rifle Division of the 3rd Army Corps, to which five soldiers killed in action were attached. The 3rd Army Corps regiment was involved in notable battles west of Klishchiivka in February 202429 and south of Chasiv Yar30 in October 2024. The highest number of deaths took place in the 7th Independent Motorised Rifle Brigade of the 3rd Army, accounting for 49 recruits killed in action. The tactics and networks of recruitment: The business of war Besides individual recruits and their fate, INPACT’s investigation was particularly interested in understanding how these men were recruited and the process of travelling to Russia to join the Russian army, identifying several tactics and mechanisms:  Travel agencies established on the continent and in Russia  The use of Kremlin intermediaries for recruitment  Influencers and fighters who act as recruitment ambassadors to their communities  Fake job postings  Illegal immigration networks The success – and resilience – of this recruitment infrastructure relies on the fact that it has generated and sustained a commercial ecosystem that has created opportunities for individuals and businesses to enrich themselves from human misery. 29 https://tsn.ua/en/ato/russia-s-new-offensive-what-is-happening-in-the-hottest-areas-of-the-front- 2517310.html 30 https://www.criticalthreats.org/analysis/russian-offensive-campaign-assessment-october-30-2024 February 2026 23 The network of travel agencies Many travel agencies based on the African continent have emerged to facilitate travel to Russia for studies, employment opportunities, or other types of stays by African nationals. INPACT identified two types of travel agencies involved in recruiting African fighters for the Russian front: agencies that advertise online explicitly for the Russian army and agencies that appear to offer only trips within Russia. A formal link with Russian authorities is often opaque. During its investigation, INPACT discovered that some travel agencies are directly accredited by the Russian authorities, and others appear to work with Russian nationals mandated by the same authorities. INPACT was able to contact Boris Alexandrovich Malikov, a Russian national who posts offers to join the Russian army on Kenyan WhatsApp job groups. We posed as an African national desperate for work. Malikov offered two options: enlist in the Russian army, promising a support role off the front lines for $2,500–$5,000 USD per month, or become his field recruiter for $500–$700 USD, paid for each contract signed within two weeks. When asked about his company's name, Malikov wrote that he operates through a shell company, “OneClickVisa,” but said that he was actually acting on behalf of the Russian Federal Security Service (FSB).
