Niger Ahmed Bello Issoufou est le président de l’organisation « Développons le Niger » qui a servi à créer la Maison Russe. 15 Il est également coordinateur communication de PARADE International , une ONG vraisemblablement créée par des postes diplomatiques russes en Afrique. PARAD signifie « Partenariat Alternatif Russie-Afrique pour le Développement Économique » et appelle à la fin des partenariats occidentaux et de la junte pro- militaire. Au Niger, PARADE a été très active lors du coup d'État militaire pour soutenir les manifestations. La Maison russe au Niger a été inaugurée en présence de Maxim Shugaley, l’ancien consultant politique de Prigozhin, qui a continué à jouer un rôle important dans les opérations d’influence sur le continent et a été accusé d’approcher les mouvements politiq ues et les médias locaux tchadiens pour diffuser des récits pro-russes. 16 En outre, la Maison Russe au Niger travaille avec une organisation dérivée de l’ African Initiative – une opération d’influence des services de sécurité russes - appelée « Ensemble Main dans la Main » dirigée par Mohamed Dicko avec un certain nombre d’événements communs ( sponsoring de football (14/07), Hygiène publique (30/07), Orphelinat (18/08), Cours sur la Russie (19/08)). Tchad Une relation similaire existe entre la Maison russe au Tchad et Parade International, puisque Youssouf Adam Abdoulah, secrétaire général de la Maison russe, était l'ancien président du mouvement mondial PARADE , qui possède également une branche au Tchad dirigée par Mahamat Issa Ali. Tout cet écosystème est amplifié par les médias d'État et les organes de propagande russes, et soutenu par les entités de soft power russes. Sénégal Le centre culturel russe Kalinka de Dakar est officiellement dirigé par Larissa Kryukova, l'épouse discrète de l'ancien ambassadeur de Russie Sergueï Kryukov. Pourtant, c'est son adjointe, Oumy Sène, fille de feu Ibrahima Sène, figure emblématique du Parti pour l'indépendance et le travail, qui pilote une grande partie de son activité et a même représenté le centre au sommet Russie- Afrique de Saint -Pétersbourg. Kalinka a également tissé un réseau éclectique de partenaires locaux, allant du ministère de l'Intérieur sénégalais à la société de sécurité privée Sagam International. 17 Oumy Sene au Forum Russie Afrique Sene est également présenté dans tous les médias de propagande russes connus, de Russia Today, les ambassades russes à African Initiative et Geopolitika. A cela s’ajoute l’existence d’une branche du KCOPC à Dakar, le Conseil des Compatriotes Russes implanté dans plusieurs pays. C’est un instrument bien documenté de la propagande russe, avec des antennes nationales dans le monde entier. Aux États-Unis, sa branche a été fermée en 2021 et, en 2022, le FBI a accusé sa fondatrice, la russo- américaine Elena Branson, de travailler pour le GRU. Dirigé par Nathalia Zhagladovna Tiam , le KCOPC de Dakar n’hésite pas à promouvoir le groupe PARADE du Sénégal pronant un renversement du système au profit d’un partenariat avec la Russie. 18 Bureau du KCOPC qui comprend en 2020 N. Tiam, V Diallo, Sene Oumy, A Fall et N Maroshkina. Représentants de Parade International avec le KCOPC 19 Parade International est établi à Dakar et présidé par Samba Mbenda DIAW, lui -même en contact avec l’ensemble de l’écosystème africain animé au travers des Maisons russes et de Africa Politology. Diaw avec Kemi Seba de Urgences Panafricanistes Urgences Panafricanistes est d’ailleurs un fort relai au Sénégal, ralliant plusieurs groupes d’activistes autour de thématique cléf pour l’influence russe comme le retrait des bases militaires françaises. 20 Pivot entre le Burkina Faso et la Côte d’Ivoire Enfin, la Maison russe au Burkina Faso joue un rôle clé dans la région et entretient des liens avec la Côte d'Ivoire. C'est là que l'influence russe 2.0 a débuté avec la création d'African Initiative BKF, l'émanation locale d'African Initiative. Autour de la Maison russe, intégrée au Centre de diplomatie publique et dirigée par Eveguenya Tikhonova, gravitent plusieurs groupes militants pro-russes locaux. Soutien Total à Vladimir Poutine en Afrique in Burkina Faso and Ivory Coast Les représentants russes (ambassade et Maison Russe) soutiennent particulièrement la SOTOVPOA. Sylvain Takoué, Ivoirien et fondateur de la SOTOVPOA (« Soutien Total à Vladimir Poutine en Afrique »), a joué un rôle clé au Burkina Faso et a défendu les intérêts de la Chine et de la Russie en Côte d'Ivoire. 21 Représentants du groupe Amitié Burkina Russie, faisant partie de SOTOVPOA SOTOVPOA regroupe plusieurs groupes comme Amitié Burkina- Russie (ABR), le Club des Jeunes pour la Promotion de la Coopération Sino- Burkinabè (CJPC -BF), Initiative pour la Coopération Economique et Financière Burkina- BRICS (ICEF-Burkina), les Frères Africains (LFA), le Collectif des leaders panafricains du Burkina Faso (CLP), Planète des Jeunes Panafricanistes (PJP), les Amazones des Cascades (ADC). L’organisation a lancé le Festival Africain Spassiba et le Prix International Vladimir Poutine pour la souveraineté culturelle en Afrique en 2023 22 Sylvain Takoué et d'autres représentants des organisations membres de la SOTOVPOA au Burkina Faso Takoué a écrit un livre sur Vladimir Poutine Guerre en Ukraine : Poutine n'est pas le mal et a ensuite rencontré l'ambassadeur russe pour préparer un festival culturel pro-russe. 23 Solidarité Panafricaniste Cote d’Ivoire En Côte d’Ivoire, la SOTOVPOA est également membre de SOPA -CI ( Solidarité Panafricaniste Côte d’Ivoire). Lancée le 24 février 2024 à Cocody, Abidjan, SOPA-CI est une plateforme regroupant plusieurs organisations de la société civile ivoirienne promouvant un discours panafricain et anti -occidental. Elle se présente comme une voix unifiée pour la souveraineté africaine à l’heure où le sentiment anti -français et pro- russe gagne du terrain dans la région. Elle est dirigée par Éric Dannon et bénéficie du soutien d’une personnalité politique ivoirienne, Ahoua Don Mello, vice-président des BRICS et historiquement proche de Laurent Gbagbo, et fondateur du Parti du peuple africain de Côte d’Ivoire (PPA-CI) en 2010. Une autre filiale, l'URPANAF-CI, est la branche ivoirienne du réseau panafricaniste créé par l'activiste Kémi Séba, proche du groupe Wagner. Selon le site web pro -Ouattara adolebatisseur.org (en référence aux initiales du président Alassane Dramane Ouattara), la SOPA-CI fonctionne comme un instrument d'influence et de renseignement au service des réseaux prorusses. Sa mission : diffuser des discours anti -occidentaux et anti -français, amplifier le discours panafricaniste et saper les partenariats internationaux de la Côte d'Ivoire. Le leader de la SOPA -CI, Eric Danon, et le leader de l'URPANAF -CI, Aka Kossougro, sont vus lors d'une manifestation appelant à la fin de la présidence de Ouattara. 24 Danon à droite (arrière-plan) et Kossougro au premier plan Les autres membres sont Alternative Citoyenne Ivoirienne (ACI), Jeunesse Panafricaine Côte d’Ivoire (JEPA-CI) et Mouvement Citoyen Panafricain Sursaut Africain (SUAF). JEPA -CI est un groupe panafricaniste basé en Côte d’Ivoire, actif principalement via une communauté Facebook, et dirigé par Danon Eric, qui en est l’administrateur principal. ARUCI en charge de la Maison Russe L'Association des Compatriotes ARUCI figure parmi les organisations partenaires sur le site web de Rossotrudnichestvo. L'association elle- même a déclaré : « Par cet accord, ARUCI a obtenu le statut de partenaire de la Maison russe. Cela lui permettra de bénéficier d'un soutien méthodologique et de partager son expérience avec les Maisons russes du monde entier. » L'ARUCI a été à l'origine de plusieurs initiatives à Abidjan, notamment la présence d'un stand russe au Salon du livre d'Abidjan et la commémoration du 80e anniversaire de la fin de la Seconde Guerre mondiale à l 'Université méthodiste de Côte d'Ivoire. Selon l'ambassade de Russie à Abidjan, l'association est présidée par Tatiana Rakitina, avec Maria Tikhonova comme secrétaire générale. 25 L'association est très active au sein du milieu universitaire ivoirien. Le 1er avril 2025, elle a organisé une présentation des cours de russe à l'Université polytechnique ISPA. Le 7 mars 2025, l'ARUCI était présente à l'EEM Business School d'Abidjan pour célébrer le lancement des premiers cours réguliers de russe dans le pays, en présence de journalistes de Russia Today. Le russe est depuis devenu une matière obligatoire à l'EEM d'Abidjan, les cours étant dispensés par Larissa Pavlovna Kurilenko, avec le soutien pédagogique de l'Université d'État de Yaroslavl, en Russie. L'ARUCI a également participé à la deuxième édition du festival artistique « J'aime la Russie » (également intitulé « La Russie, mon amour »), qui s'est tenu du 3 au 5 juin 2025 à l'Université UMECI d'Abidjan. La première édition s'est tenue en 2024, également à l'UMECI. Le festival a été financé par la Fondation « Mon Histoire » avec le soutien de l'ambassade de Russie en Côte d'Ivoire, dont l'ambassadeur Alexeï Saltykov a prononcé les discours d'ouverture et de clôture. Le président de l'UMECI, Makagnon René Gnaléga, était également présent. Au moins quatre professeurs russes de l'Université d'État de Iaroslavl étaient présents : Elena Boychuk, Lyudmila Titova, Danil Svy atokho, Elizaveta Antipova et Yulia Butko. Boychuk est identifiée comme la principale personne- ressource universitaire et l'organisatrice en chef du festival. Il convient de noter que Yulia Butko a précédemment enseigné à l'Université pédagogique d'État du Donbass à Sloviansk, en Ukraine. On retrouve aussi le KCPOC en Côte d’Ivoire, main dans la main avec le centre culturel russe. Officiellement, le site web de la KCOPC ne mentionne pas de branche en Côte d'Ivoire, citant uniquement l'ARUCI et sa présidente Tatiana Rakitina comme point de contact local. Pourtant, l'ambassade de Russie à Abidjan a annoncé la tenue de la première conférence de la KCOPC en Côte d'Ivoire le 7 mars 2025, précisant que l'organisation avait été créée en 2024. L'ARUCI a confirmé sa participation sur les réseaux sociaux en publiant une photo de sa présidente Tatiana Rakitina assise aux côtés de l'ambassadeur Saltykov. 26 Les choix des cadres des Maisons Russes franchisées issus de mouvements politiques actifs en faveur du changement de régime dans leur pays respectif et militant agressivement contre le système occidental posent donc la question des réelles intentions d’un opérateur culturel étranger comme les Maisons Russes dans les pays d’accueil. Par ailleurs, l’implantation de ces centres stimulent en réalité un écosystème d’organisations activistes faisant la promotion des intérêts de la Russie dans son sillage et des partenariats avec des organisations russes créées par les services de sécurité russes. Pas la promotion de la culture, mais de la guerre La programmation “culturelle” des maisons russes à travers le monde, et notamment par les centres franchisés dans les zones d'influence clés de la Russie comme l'Afrique, est remarquable. Au lieu de programmer des cycles de films consacrés à l'œuvre de Sergueï Eisenstein ou aux peintures de Kandinsky, les maisons russes ont privilégié les événements militaires et lié s à la guerre. En République centrafricaine, le film « Turist », financé par Prigojine, promouvant le travail du Groupe Wagner dans le pays, a été projeté en mai 2022. Les films présentés au public local sont essentiellement des films de guerre historiques vantant le rôle de la Russie et de l'Union soviétique dans la paix mondiale. 27 Les visites de représentants militaires dans les maisons russes sont courantes, comme l'événement autour d'un navire de la marine en Tanzanie. 28 Événement de la Maison russe en Tanzanie autour de l'escale de la flotte de la marine à Dar Es Salam Les cours de russe proposés ne sont pas annoncés par l'ambassade de Russie ou un représentant du ministère de la Culture mais par nul autre que le vice-ministre de la Défense Yunus Bek Yevkurov , lors d'une de ses visites régulières dans la capitale nigérienne de Niamey. 29 Un amplificateur des opérations d’influence de la Russie Les Maisons russes en Afrique fonctionnent de moins en moins comme des centres culturels et davantage comme des amplificateurs des opérations d'influence plus vastes de Moscou. Loin d'être des avant-postes neutres de la diplomatie culturelle, elles opèrent en tandem avec African Initiative – une organisation soutenue par Moscou, largement perçue par les analystes comme liée à l'appareil sécuritaire russe et sanctionnée par l'Union européenne – et un ensemble hétéroclite de partenaires locaux pour organiser des événements, mobiliser le public et cultiver des réseaux de sympathisants. Leurs activités vont au-delà des simples manœuvres de soft power. Les Maisons alimentent directement l'écosystème de propagande du Kremlin, veillant à ce que les interventions de personnalités politiques, universitaires ou citoyennes africaines soient relayées par des médias contrôlés par l'État comme Sputnik et RT, où elles sont présentées comme des preuves de la légitimité et de la portée de la Russie sur le continent. RT est allée plus loin avec son « RT Academy », un programme de formation au journalisme adapté à l'Afrique, qui, selon ses critiques, brouille la frontière entre éducation aux médias et conditionnement idéologique. Il a été également suggéré que certaines de ces initiatives impliquent la diffusion de contenus visant à jeter le doute sur les médias internationaux, présentant des médias comme la BBC, CNN ou France 24 comme des instruments du néocolonialisme occidental. En alliant programmation culturelle, communication médiatique et partenariats stratégiques, les Maisons russes se positionnent non seulement comme des pôles culturels bienveillants, mais aussi comme des nœuds actifs au sein d'un réseau d'influence plus va ste cherchant à repenser les discours sur le pouvoir et la légitimité en Afrique. 30 La RT Academy donne un cours à la Maison russe du Mali sur la guerre de l'information, affirmant que CNN, BBC et DW publient de fausses nouvelles et intoxiquent les gens. Le réseau des Maisons Russes au service du recrutement pou r le complexe militaro-industriel et la guerre en Ukraine D'après les documents reçus par l'INPACT, la Russie aurait recruté environ 1407 ressortissants africains pour combattre en Ukraine au sein de l'armée russe. Bien qu'il soit impossible à ce stade d'affirmer que toutes les Maisons russes sont des lieux de recrutement, elles jouent un rôle important pour le programme militaire russe. Le réseau des Maisons russes a été utilisé pour promouvoir et recruter de la main- d'œuvre pour le programme de démarrage d'Alabuga, qui fournit du personnel à la zone économique spéciale d'Alabuga, où est implanté le programme de fabrication de drones en c ollaboration avec l'Iran. Ce programme a été présenté en Zambie, en Tanzanie, au Kirghizistan et en Inde. 31 Événement de recrutement à la Maison russe de Zambie 32 Publicités par plusieurs Maisons Russes 33 Récemment, en avril 2025, le programme a organisé un concours à la Maison Russe de RCA avec des participants photographiés avec des goodies Alabuga. Événement de recrutement à la Maison russe de RCA Au Kirghizistan, Viktor Loukovenko, ancien membre de l'Initiative africaine et de l'appareil d'influence Prigojine, a été arrêté par les autorités locales , accusé une fois de plus d'avoir utilisé sa position au sein de la Maison russe locale pour tenter de recruter des mercenaires locaux dans les villes d'Och et de Bakten afin de combattre aux côtés des forces russes en Ukraine. Les migrants kirghizes, longtemps maltraités en Russie, se sont retrouvés pour certains sur le front ukrainien, principalement ceux originaires de la région de Batken. Plusieurs employés de la Maison russe d'Och ont également été arrêtés, suscitant une nouvelle fois les condamnations des médias d'influence russes. Espionnage et culture, un cercle pas si vertueux? Malgré sa vocation officielle de réseau d'influence culturelle, le réseau des Maisons Russes a régulièrement été accusé – et impliqué – dans des activités d'espionnage et des tentatives présumées d'ingérence dans les écosystèmes et la dynamique politiques et communicationnelles des pays ciblés. 34 Cela est particulièrement vrai pour les anciennes républiques soviétiques, comme l'Azerbaïdjan et le Kirghizistan, où des Maisons Russes ont été fermées et certains de leurs membres arrêtés. À Bakou, les autorités locales ont arrêté un citoyen azéri travaillant pour la Maison Russe locale, Ivan Nikolaïevitch Svetlichny, accusé d'espionnage. La Maison Russe locale a ensuite été fermée, suscitant de nombreuses condamnations de la part des médias d'influence russes. Alors qu'en Afrique, les Maisons Russes semblent bénéficier d'un environnement opérationnel plus permissif, un « politologue » russe réputé, fort d'une longue expérience en matière d'ingérence politique, a également été arrêté à son arrivée à N'Djamena, au Tchad. Maxim Shugaley et son traducteur Samir Sufyan sont des « sociologues » travaillant pour la Fondation pour la défense des valeurs nationales. Il a participé à des sondages auprès des populations de pays qui intéressaient la Russie et le groupe Wagner, comme le Soudan, la Libye, le Mali et la RCA. Il a travaillé pour l'empire Prigozhin à Madagascar et en Libye, où il a été arrêté en mai 2019 après avoir rencontré Seif al-Islam Kadhafi, le fils de l'ancien dirigeant libyen Mouhammar Kadhafi. Il a été libéré après plusieurs mois, tandis que l'agence de propagande Prigozhin a ensuite sorti un film d'action, intitulé à juste titre « Shugaley », relatant les aventures de son conseiller en communication en Libye. Maxim Shugaley et Samir Sufyan ont été arrêtés à leur arrivée à l 'aéroport international de N'Djamena le 19 septembre 2024. Les motifs exacts de ces arrestations restent inconnus, mais des informations suggèrent que Shugaley et Sufyan, en visite officielle au Tchad pour assister à l'inauguration de la Maison russe locale, ont été arrêtés par les services de renseignement nationaux tchadiens, soupçonnés de projeter des activités d 'ingérence politique. Selon un document judiciaire consulté par INPACT, Shugaley et Sufyan étaient en charge de toutes les communications autour de l'ouverture de la Maison russe et ont été désignés par certaines personnes impliquées dans l'affaire judiciaire comme ceux qui ont ouvert le centre culturel à Farcha. Accélérer les ouvertures, le meilleur vecteur pour de l’influence durable et l’engagement militaire Les Maisons russes sont présentes sur la plupart des continents, de l'Europe à l'Afrique en passant par les continents américain et asiatique. Le réseau semble en constante expansion en Afrique, parallèlement aux autres offensives d'influence menées par le Kremlin. À cet égard, le développement des Maisons russes met en lumière l'importance de la stratégie d'influence russe. De nouveaux partenariats et de futures ouvertures ont été régulièrement annoncés depuis fin 2024 et 2025, généralement par l'intermédiaire d'associations et de centres déjà existants. C'est notamment le cas en Somalie et en Sierra Leone, où les annonces ont été faites respectivement par le Centre Pouchkine et la Fondation pour le patrimoine russe. Les Maisons russes sont devenues des pôles d'attraction autour desquels gravitent les médias pro-Kremlin, les influenceurs et les proxies, amplifiant les discours anti-occidentaux et 35 ancrant le message de Moscou au sein des communautés locales. Loin d'être de simples caisses de résonance, ces avant-postes franchisés ressemblent de plus en plus à des centres de recrutement pour la machine de guerre russe. On est loin du mandat traditionnel de la diplomatie culturelle. Les institutions qui étaient autrefois censées mettre en valeur la langue, l’histoire et l’art de la Russie ont été remodelées, manipulées et militarisées, transformées de vitrines du soft power en instruments de hard power.

1xIVNZj-Zfjs4kyIW0S3MJ4-xLnUH5wRp.text: F R O M D O S T O Y E V S K Y T O D R O N E S T R I K E S : T H E D O U B L E L I F E O F T H E R U S S I A N H O U S E S 1 From Dostoevsky to Drone Strikes: The Double Life of Russian Houses Written by INPACT December 2025 contact@investig-impact.org Follow us on social media @aeowinpact 2 BLUF Russian Houses – Russian cultural centres which are traditionally instruments of state soft power promoting language and culture - have increasingly been privatised and franchised to Russian organisations. These entities rely on nationals in countries of strategic importance to Moscow, who are frequently recruited from manufactured anti West activist groups sponsored by Russia and implicated in covert influence operations. Moreover, these houses have functioned as recruitment halls and amplified military propaganda, bolstering Russia's war effort. INPACT’s key findings: - Rossotrudnichestvo, the state agency in charge of promoting culture and humanitarian affairs, is relying on a mix of entities to expand its network of cultural centres: branches, partnership with Russian private organisations and a private military company to open Russian Houses, local companies and local associations. - Franchised Russian Houses are part of Africa Politology ’s strategy for the continent which is in charge of offensive and clandestine influence operations on be half of the Russian State: the entity is financing their operation costs throughout the continent - This franchised model enables the Russian state to go faster at expanding their influence network but also dilutes responsibilities and roles to non-state actors. - Most management teams are recruited among the local civil society, often from anti - western activist groups sponsored by Russian embassies and advocating for regime change in the host country. - Russian Houses have become a focal point around which a local anti -Western ecosystem aligned on Russian worldview and often politically motivated for regime change can gravitate. - Russian Houses are promoting both Russia’s culture and history and war -themed events and the Russian military. They acts as physical echo- chambers in country to amplify Russian led influence operations like African Initiative and its local branches. - Russian Houses have also become halls for recruitments and covert operations with aggressive recruitment for the Russian militaro-complex and espionnage taking place there. Recommendations: - Franchised Russian Houses displaying an inappropriate activity involving recruitment for military industries and the army or espionage operations should be closed and/or sanctioned by host country - Russian ambassadors in host countries should be questioned and made accountable for the intentions and activities of franchised Russian Houses led by nationals openly pro-destabilisation and calling for regime change. 3 An innocent game Last May, in Bangui, young Central Africans gathered at the Russian House, a company incorporated in the Central African Republic in 2021 and managed by Dimitri Sytyi, a key executive of the Wagner group and the notorious Internet Research Agency in St. Petersburg. Their purpose was not to engage with Russian literature or attend language classes. Instead, diligently working on laptops, they were participating in the " Business Cats Entrepreneurship Olympiad", a competition designed to identify the most financially literate individuals. This event was organised by Alabuga Start, a recruitment program launched across Africa to enlist labour, particularly young women, for Russia’s military industrial efforts, specifically a Shahed drone factory. After the competition, participants were phot ographed with their Alabuga start goodies bags. They also harbored hopes of job prospects outside their home country, a region where Russia has historically pursued an aggressive influence agenda. The selection of the Russian House as the venue for these recruitment events, particularly for an industry vital to the war effort, is not accidental. Russian Houses now serve a dual function: promoting Pushkin on one hand, and facilitating military engagement and offensive influence actions on the other. Source: Facebook 4 A common instrument of soft power under scrutiny Like a lot of other nations, Russia has been developing instruments of soft power to expand its influence world wide trying to seduce rather than coerce using culture, values and foreign policies to shape the preferences of others. Russian Houses which are cultural centres created by the Russian Federal Agency for the Affairs of the Commonwealth of Independent States (Rossotrudnichestvo), a Russian state agency, are a prime example of such instruments. Direct heir of the “Russian Centres for Culture and Sciences”, Rossotrudnichestvo, created in 2008 by Presidential Decree, aims at developing the positive perception of Russia and the Russian population, culture and history across the world, and most specifically in Africa, where the number of local Russian Houses has been rapidly increasing. The organisation is headed by Evgeny Primakov, who has had a career in journalism and state positions including in a state communication technology company. He is reported to be very close to Vladimir Putin and is the grandson of Evgueny Primakov, ex Director of the SVR, Russia External Intelligence Services. Rossotrudnichestvo has officially opened 87 Russian Houses in the world including 6 in Africa. Map displayed on Rossotrudnichestvo’s website As many cultural centres, like the British Council, Goethe Institute or Alliance Française,the official activities of the network of Russian Houses worldwide officially revolves around the promotion of Russian culture, education, arts and history. In Afric a, Russian Houses also organise courses aimed at teaching local students Russian language. This is notably the case in Niger, Burkina Faso and Mali. 5 Russian houses are also the window on secondary education partnerships with scholarships proposed to beneficiaries where Russian houses are established. Universities like the Patrice Lumumba Peoples' Friendship University of Russia (RUDN) have been aggress ively concluding partnerships with African and Asian universities in the past few months. Other activities include the organisation of historical and art exhibitions, and in countries such as Burkina Faso and Niger, the delivery of limited quantities of humanitarian supplies, most notably school books and other educational material. Dimitri Sytyi/ Russian House in CAR distributing humanitarian supplies to the population (local newspapers) With the start of the war in Ukraine, Rossotrudnitchestvo has been strongly scrutinised and placed on international Western sanctions lists in 2023 for supporting the war in Ukraine and using its global networks to disseminate propaganda and aggressive anti-Western narratives. Despite sanctions, the Kyiv Independent highlighted that Russian Houses continue to operate across Europe, including in Brussels, Berlin, Paris, Rome, Madrid, Luxemburg, Vienna, Prague, Warsaw, Budapest, Chisinau, Bratislava. Yet it has not prevented the agency from developing more in Africa with multiple openings in recent months. Beyond a war of narratives and counter narrative, INPACT has found an unusual organisation mixing armed groups and private actors as well as a clear goal to convince and enable covert and military related activities. 6 The grey zone: a network of franchised cultural centres managed by private actors and transnational criminal organisation Rossotrudnichestvo has chosen a curious development path by diplomatic standards to increase the rate of openings of cultural centres and Russian Houses based on franchising the Russian Houses allowing other organisations to open and manage them. In a short post on Telegram from blogger and GRU linked Viktor Lukovenko, Rossotrudnichestvo’s franchise strategy is seen as giving equal chances to private actors to operate in Africa. Recently the agency has unified the branding of all the Russian houses signaling an endorsement but making it difficult to distinguish the official network from the franchised network. New logo Their first partner to implement this franchise strategy was the Wagner group headed by Evgeny Prigozhin and listed as a terrorist organisation or transnational criminal organisation in many jurisdictions. Incorporated as a Single member Limited Liability Company (SURL) by Dimitri Sytyi, a key executive of the Wagner Group influence arm in CAR, in June 2021, it was the first Russian House outside the direct ownership and management of Rossotrudnichestvo. 7 Letter issued by Dimitri Sytyi showing it is a company - source: social media This first franchise was actually one of the last places Evgeny Prigozhin visited before dying in his plane in August 2023. Evgeny Prigozhin at the Russian House in CAR - August 2023 Since January 2024, the Centre for Public Diplomacy which was actually legally registered as an organisation in February 2024 has opened a Russian House in Burkina Faso. It is directed by three persons: Natalia Krasosvkaya (doctor in psychology working at the Duma), Leonid Isaev (Director of an academic centre at the High School of Economics) and Galina Guerasimova. According to Krasovskaya’s Telegram account, the Centre for Public Diplomacy 8 signed an official agreement with Rossotrudnichestvo to open “partner Russian Houses” in the Sahel and Africa in April 2024. In the centre Evgueny Primakov (head of Rosstrudnichestvo), Dmitry Savelyev (State Duma) and Natalia Krasovskaya (Centre for Public Diplomacy), Leonid Isaev (Centre for Public Diplomacy) Since then the Centre has opened Russian Houses in Ouagadougou in Burkina Faso, in Bamako, in Niamey, Niger in April 2025 and in Conakry, Guinea in July 2025 and plans for Mali. Signature for an agreement for cooperation between Rossotrudnichestvo and representatives of private organisations for Russian Houses in September 2024. Dimitri Sytyi can be spotted. 9 Other forms and organisations have opened Russian houses on the continent. In Equatorial Guinea, in Malabo, it is a private local company used to set up the Russian Cultural Centre. In some countries, local associations have been set up to open Russian Hou ses like the Pushkin Centre in Somalia, Russian Heritage Fondation in Sierra Leone, the association of compatriots ARUCI for Cote d’Ivoire, Fraternity Madagascar-Russia, Developpons le Niger or Teriya Mali-Ressie in Mali. Photo of the franchised Russian House in Mali, lacking branding alignment (source AFRINZ) Multiple actors investing in the Russian House sector means several houses can open in one country managed by different teams with different agendas. The legal structures also raise questions on their immunity to local laws and international rights as well as legitimacy in representing the Russian state or the opportunity for Russia to put some sort of plausible deniability or distance themselves from actions difficult to endorse. Amplifying and enabling covert and military influence operations But what sets Russian houses apart from other foreign cultural centres and is questionable is their activities as part of covert operations. In that context, Russian houses are part and parcel of the hybrid warfare Russia is currently waging and aiming at reshaping world views, capturing resources for the war and discrediting countries labelled as Russia’s enemies. At the heart of Africa Politology’s strategy for the continent A new Russian house was inaugurated on July 12, 2025, in Conakry by the Center for Public Diplomacy. According to INPACT sources, this was quickly made possible by persistent lobbying by the Africa Politology network, which works directly with the Russian foreign intelligence service, the SVR. This is not without reason: confidential documents on the strategy and campaigns of this organization, which INPACT was able to consult, show that the creation of "Non-Governmental Russian Houses" is one of the action points promoted by 10 Africa Politology. The organization allocates a budget of USD 10,000 to 20,000 per month for the one in Mali. Africa Politology was born from the Wagner Group's work in Africa within the Lakhta project. Comprised of communications and public affairs consultants, sociologists, lawyers, translators, and lobbyists, the organization is particularly active in Libya, Sudan, Mali, Burkina Faso, Niger, Chad, Guinea Conakry, Togo, Ivory Coast, the Central African Republic, the Democratic Republic of Congo, Namibia, Angola, and South Africa. It conducts sociological studies in target countries, builds networks of informants, and organizes various demonstrations and public events. It promotes the interests of Russia, and more specifically those of Wagner. According to INPACT sources, Africa Politology's missions revolve around four pillars: research and monitoring of African c ountries, influence campaigns and electoral strategy proposals, demonstrations and lobbying, providing advice and recommendations to African authorities, and the production of legal documents such as proposed laws or memoranda of understanding. The management team is composed of former members of Prigozhin's back office and primarily includes political scientists and sociologists. 11 From rather sensitive sources, Africa Politology has recently distinguished itself by supporting Russian firms and by exerting influence in several countries: - In Côte d'Ivoire: Africa Politology sought to discredit candidate Alassane Dramane Ouattara by mobilizing a network of activist groups presented below as part of the presidential election campaign. - In Niger: It established a network of paid sources in the country and made contact with pro-Russian Nigerien figures, such as advisors in ministerial offices, political party leaders, and civil society leaders, who were paid in sums of approximately €500. - In Mali and Burkina Faso: It lobbied to pass a bill on foreign agents, modeled on the Russian model. These laws are actually aimed at controlling and preventing any form of opposition to the government. - In CAR: Africa Politology assisted in the negotiations of a border agreement between Chad and the Central African Republic. A web of Pro -Russian politically motivated influencers heading Russian Houses The business model for Russian houses does not rely only on partnerships with private actors or local organisations and companies but also on individuals sold to the Kremlin’s worldview and members of Pro-Russian organisations very close to local Russian embassies. The goal is to ensure a deep- rooted cooperation with key local stakeholders who are well established in their environment and sold to the Kremlin’s worldviews. But what is striking is that these individuals are coming from activist groups politically oriented, far from culture and art and with a political agenda centred around regime change. With them onboard, the Russian Houses become focal points to develop and entertain a local ecosystem favourable or aligned on the Kremlin’s positions and narratives and which are positioned as opposition forces to their national system and supporters of military juntas. Mali In Mali, the Russian House chairman, Aliou Tounkara, is a member of the Malian National Transition Council. He has been a key person with whom Russia has been engaging: featured 12 on Russia Today or Sputnik. He has also been invited as a guest of honour to the Russia Africa summit sumit in 2023. His statements are often re-used by Russian official bodies. Tounkara is the key entry point to the Malian diaspora in Russia as he is the president of the Council of Malians in the Russian Federation. This is important not only because the Russian House in Mali is establishing partnerships with universities for student exchanges but also because 38 Malians have enrolled in the Russian army since 2024 to serve on the battleground in Ukraine based on a confidential document INPACT has obtained and is investigating. 13 This enables other individuals and organisations to gravitate around him and the Russian House. Representatives of the Malian government like Oumar Diallo, the Prime Minister Chief of Staff, or Mariam Diarra Savané, Deputy Head of Parliament's International Affairs Committee, are attending events at the Russian House. But also, Russian organisations closely related to the Russian in security services like African Initiative and the Sahelian Perspective, headed by Malian blogger Mahmadou Bah, are present. 14 Finally the Russian House in Mali is also working with local activist groups aligned with Moscow’s views like Le Groupe des Patriotes du Mali (the Malian Patriotic Group) which is very straightforward about its allegiance. Niger Ahmed Bello Issoufou is the President of the organisation “Developpons le Niger” which has been used to create the Russian House. He is also the communication coordinator of PARADE International, a NGO most likely set up by Russian diplomatic posts in Africa. PARAD stands for “Partenariat Alternatif Russie Afrique pour le Développement Economique (Russian Africa Alternative Partnership for Economic Development) calling for the end of Western part nerships and pro- military junta. In Niger PARADE was very active at the time of the military coup to support demonstrations. Bello is also an ex member of the organisation Tournons la Page 15 The Russian House in Niger was inaugurated with Maxim Shugaley, Prigozhin’s ex political consultant who has kept playing an important role in influence operations on the continent and has been accused of approaching Chadian local political movements and media to spin pro- Russian narratives. 16 In addition the Russian House in Niger is working with a spinoff organisation of African Initiative called “Ensemble Main dans la Main“ headed by Mohamed Dicko with a number of joint events (Football sponsoring (14/07), Public Hygiene (30/07), Orphanage (18/08), Courses on Russia (19/08)). Chad A similar relation exists between the Russian house in Chad and Parade International as Youssouf Adam Abdoulah, General Secretary of the Russian House, was the ex President of the global PARADE movement which also has a branch in Chad headed by Mahamat Issa Ali. All this ecosystem is amplified by Russian state media or propaganda outlets and supported by Russia’s soft power entities. Senegal The Russian cultural centre Kalinka in Dakar is formally headed by Larissa Kryukova, the low- profile wife of former Russian ambassador Sergei Kryukov. Yet it is her deputy, Oumy Sene — daughter of the late Ibrahima Sene, a leading figure in the Party for Independence and Labour — who drives much of its activity and even represented the centre at the Russia–Africa summit in St Petersburg. Kalinka has also cultivated an eclectic network of local partners, ranging from Senegal’s interior ministry to the private security company Sagam International. Oumy Sene at the Russia Africa Forum Sene is also featured in all known Russian propaganda outlet from Russia Today, Russian Embassies to African Initiative and Geopolitika. Added to this is the existence of a Dakar branch of the KCOPC, the Council of Russian Compatriots, which has offices in several countries. It is a well-documented instrument of 17 Russian propaganda, with national branches around the world. In the United States, its branch was shut down in 2021, and in 2022, the FBI accused its founder, Russian-American Elena Branson, of working for the GRU. Led by Nathalia Zhagladovna Tiam, the Dakar KCOPC does not hesitate to promote the Senegalese group PARADE, which advocates an overthrow of the system in favor of a partnership with Russia. KCOPC office which in 2020 includes N. Tiam, V Diallo, Sene Oumy, A Fall and N Maroshkina. 18 Representatives of Parade International with the KCOPC Parade International is based in Dakar and chaired by Samba Mbenda DIAW, himself in contact with the entire African ecosystem animated through the Russian Houses and Africa Politology. 19 Diaw with Kemi Seba from Panafricanist Emergencies Urgences Panafricanistes is also a strong relay in Senegal, rallying several activist groups around key themes for Russian influence such as the withdrawal of French military bases. 20 Pivot between Burkina Faso and Ivory Coast Finally the Russian House in Burkina Faso plays a key role in the region and has links with Cote d’Ivoire. This is where the Russian influence 2.0 started with the creation of African Initiation BKF, the local spinoff of African Initiative. Gathered around the Russian house incorporated by the Centre for Public Diplomacy and headed by Eveguenya Tikhonova, a number of local pro Russian activist groups gravitate. Soutien Total à Vladimir Poutine en Afrique in Burkina Faso and Ivory Coast Russian representatives (embassy and Maison Russe) specifically support SOTOVPOA. Sylvain Takoué who is Ivoirian and founded SOTOVPOA (« Soutien Total à Vladimir Poutine en Afrique ») has been a key force in Burkina Faso and has been advocating for both China and Russia back in Ivory Coast. 21 Representatives of Amitié Burkina Russie, part of SOTOVPOA SOTOVPOA which gathers several groups like Amitié Burkina- Russie (ABR), le Club des Jeunes pour la Promotion de la Coopération Sino- Burkinabè (CJPC -BF), Initiative pour la Coopération Economique et Financière Burkina- BRICS (ICEF-Burkina), les Frères Africains (LFA), le Collectif des leaders panafricains du Burkina Faso (CLP), Planète des Jeunes Panafricanistes (PJP), les Amazones des Cascades (ADC) launched the African Spassiba Festival and the Vladimir Putin International Prize for cultural sovereignty in Africa in 2023 22 Sylvain Takoué and other representatives of organisations part of SOTOVPOA in Burkina Faso Takoué has written a book on Vladimir Putin The War in Ukraine: Putin is Not Evil and subsequently met the Russian ambassador to prepare a pro-Russian cultural festival. 23 Solidarité Panafricaniste Cote d’Ivoire In Ivory Coast, SOTOVPOA is also a member of SOPA -CI (Solidarité Panafricaniste Cote d’Ivoire). Launched on 24 February 2024 in Cocody, Abidjan, SOPA-CI is a platform bringing together several Ivorian civil society organisations promoting a pan- African, anti -Western narrative. It presents itself as a unified voice for African sovereignty at a time when anti-French and pro- Russian sentiment is gaining traction in the region. It is led by Eric Dannon and receives the support of an Ivoirian political figure, Ahoua Don Mello, vice -president of the BRICS and historically close to Laurent Gbagbo and creator of the political party Parti du peuple africain de Côte d’Ivoire (PPA-CI) in 2010 . 24 Another affiliate, URPANAF-CI, is the Ivorian branch of the pan-Africanist network created by activist Kémi Séba, close to the Wagner group. According to the pro- Ouattara website adolebatisseur.org (a reference to President Alassane Dramane Ouattara’s initials), SOPA-CI functions as an influence and intelligence instrument serving pro- Russian networks. Its mission: to spread anti-Western and anti-French narratives, amplify pan-Africanist discourse, and undermine Côte d’Ivoire’s international partnerships. SOPA-CI leader, Eric Danon, and URPANAF -CI leader, Aka Kossougro, are seen at demonstration calling for the end of Ouattara presidency. 25 Danon on the right (background) and Kossougro on the foreground The other members are Alternative Citoyenne Ivoirienne (ACI), Jeunesse Panafricaine Côte d’Ivoire (JEPA-CI) and Mouvement Citoyen Panafricain Sursaut Africain (SUAF). JEPA-CI est un groupe panafricaniste basé en Côte d’Ivoire, actif principalement via une communauté Facebook, et mené par Danon Eric, qui en est l’administrateur principal. ARUCI in charge of the Russian House Listed as a partner organisation on the website of Rossotrudnichestvo, The Association of Compatriots ARUCI. The association itself has declared: “Under this agreement, ARUCI has obtained partner status with the Russian House. This will allow it to benefit from methodological support and to share its experience with Russian Houses worldwide.” ARUCI has been behind several initiatives in Abidjan, including the presence of a Russian stand at the Abidjan Book Fair and the commemoration of the 80th anniversary of the end of the Second World War at the Methodist University of Côte d’Ivoire. According to the Russian embassy in Abidjan, the association is chaired by Tatiana Rakitina, with Maria Tikhonova as secretary general. 26 The association appears highly active within Ivorian academia. On 1 April 2025, it organised a presentation of Russian language courses at ISPA Polytechnic University. On 7 March 2025, ARUCI was at EEM Business School in Abidjan to celebrate the launch of the first regular Russian language courses in the country, in the presence of Russia Today journalists. Russian has since been made a compulsory subject at EEM Abidjan, with classes taught by Larissa Pavlovna Kurilenko, supported pedagogically by Yaroslavl State University in Russia. ARUCI also took part in the second edition of the “I Love Russia” arts festival (also branded “Russia, My Love”), held from 3 to 5 June 2025 at UMECI University in Abidjan. The first edition took place in 2024, also at UMECI. The festival was financed by the My History Foundation with support from the Russian embassy in Côte d’Ivoire, whose ambassador Alexey Saltykov delivered the opening and closing speeches. UMECI president Makagnon René Gnaléga attended as well. At least four Russian lecturers from Yaroslavl State University were present: Elena Boychuk, Lyudmila Titova, Danil Svyatokho, Elizaveta Antipova and Yulia Butko. Boychuk is identified as the main academic contact and “chief organiser” of the festival. Notably, Yulia Butko previously taught at Donbas State Pedagogical University in Sloviansk, Ukraine. The KCOPC is also present in Ivory Coast. Officially, the KCOPC website does not list a branch in Côte d’Ivoire, citing only ARUCI and its president Tatiana Rakitina as its local point of contact. Yet the Russian embassy in Abidjan announced the holding of the first KCOPC conference in Côte d’Ivoire on 7 March 2025, stating the organisation had been created in 2024. ARUCI confirmed on social media that it participated, posting a photograph of its president Rakitina seated next to Ambassador Saltykov. 27 The choice of managers for franchised Russian Houses, drawn from political movements actively advocating regime change in their respective countries and aggressively opposing the Western system, raises questions about the true intentions of a foreign cultural operator like the Russian Houses in their host countries. Furthermore, the establishment of these centres actually fosters an ecosystem of activist organizations promoting Russian interests and establishing partnerships with Russian organizations created by the Russian security services. Not promoting culture, but war The “cultural” programmation of Russian houses across the world and especially by franchised cultural centres in Russia’s key zones of influence like Africa is striking. Instead of programming film cycles on Sergey Eisentein’s work or Kandinsky’s paintings, Russian houses have been strongly focusing on military and war related events. In the Central African Republic, the Prigozhin- funded movie “Turist”, promoting the work of Wagner Group in the country, was screened in May 2022. The movies presented to local audiences are essentially historical war movies praising the role of Russia and the Soviet Union in ensuring world peace. 28 Visits of military representatives in Russian houses are common like the event around a navy ship in Tanzania. Russian House event in Tanzania around the navy fleet stopping by Dar Es Salam 29 Russian classes offerings are not announced by the Russian embassy or a Ministry of Culture representative but by no one else than Deputy Minister of Defense Yunus Bek Yevkurov , during one of his regular visits to the Nigerien capital of Niamey. An amplifier of Russia’s influence operations Russian Houses in Africa have increasingly functioned less as cultural centres and more as amplifiers of Moscow’s broader influence operations. Far from being neutral outposts of cultural diplomacy, they operate in tandem with African Initiative — a Moscow -backed organisation widely viewed by analysts as linked to Russia’s security apparatusand sanctioned by the European Union — and a patchwork of local partners to stage events, mobilise audiences, and cultivate networks of sympathisers. Their activities extend beyond soft-power gestures. The Houses feed directly into the Kremlin’s propaganda ecosystem, ensuring that appearances by African political, academic or civic figures are channelled into state- controlled outlets such as Sputnik and RT, where they are repackaged as evidence of Russia’s legitimacy and reach on the continent. RT has gone further with its “RT Academy”, a training scheme offering journalism courses tailored for Africa, which critics say blurs the line between media education and ideological conditioning. Reports suggest that some of these initiatives also involve circulating material designed to cast doubt on international media, framing outlets such as the BBC, CNN or France 24 as tools of Western neo- colonialism. By weaving cultural programming with medi a spin and strategic partnerships, Russian Houses are positioning themselves not merely as benign cultural hubs, but as active nodes in a broader influence network seeking to reframe narratives of power and legitimacy in Africa. 30 RT Academy giving a class at the Malian Russian House about information warfare arguing that CNN, BBC and DW are publishing fake news and intoxicating people Network to recruit labour for the military -industrial complex and the war in Ukraine Based on documents INPACT received, Russia would have recruited around 990 African nationals to combat in Ukraine in the Russian army. While it is too much at this stage to assert all Russian Houses are places where recruitments are taking place, they do play an important role for the Russian military programme. The Russian houses network has been used to advertise and recruit labour for the Alabuga start programme which is staffing the Alabuga special economic zone on which is established the drone manufacturing programme in collaboration with Iran. The programme has been adversited in Zambia, Tanzania, Kyrgyzstan and in India. 31 Recruitment event at the Russian House in Zambia 32 Advertisement by different Russian Houses Recently, in April 2025, the programme organised a competition in the CAR Russia House with participants pictured with Alabuga goodies. 33 Recruitment event at the Russian House in CAR In Kyrgyzstan, Viktor Lukovenko, a former member of the African Initiative and of the Prigozhin influence apparatus, was detained by local authorities , again upon accusations of using his position with the local Russian House to attempt to recruit local mercenaries in the town of Osh and Bakten to fight alongside Russian forces in Ukraine. Kyrgyz migrants who have long been badly treated in Russia have ended up for some on the battle front in Ukraine, mainly those coming from the Batken region. Several employees of the Russian House in Osh were also detained, again prompting condemnations from Russian influence media outlets. Espionage and culture, a not-so virtuous circle? Despite its official raison d’etre as a cultural influence network, the Russian House network has regularly been accused of - and involved - in espionage activities and alleged attempts to interfere within the political and communication ecosystems and dynamics of the targeted countries. This is specifically true for former Soviet Republics, such as Azerbaijan and Kyrgyzstan, where Russian Houses were respectively closed and some of their members arrested. In Baku, local authorities arrested an Azeri citizen working with the local Russian House, Ivan Nikolayevich 34 Svetlichny, upon accusations of espionage. The local Russian House was later closed, prompting widespread condemnations from Russian influence media. Whilst in Africa, Russian Houses appear to enjoy a more permissive operating environment, a known Russian “politologist”, with a long-standing background in political interferences, was also detained upon arrival in N’Djamena, Chad. Maxim Shugaley, along with his translator Samir Sufyan, is a “sociologist” working for the Foundation for the Defence of National Values. He has been involved in polling populations of countries in which Russia and the Wagner group were interested in like Sudan, Libya, Mali, CAR. He has worked for the Prigozhin empire in Madagascar and in Libya, where he was arrested in May 2019 after meeting with the son of former Libyan leader Muhammar Qaddafi, Seif-al-Islam Gaddafi. He was released after several months, whilst the Prigozhin propaganda arm later released an action movie, adequately named “Shugaley”, depicting the adventures of its spin doctor in Libya. Maxim Shugaley and Samir Sufyan were arrested upon arrival at N’Djamena international airport on 19th September 2024. The exact motives behind the arrests remain unknown, however reporting suggests Shugaley and Sufyan, who officially visited Chad to attend the opening of the local Russian House, were detained by Chad’s national intelligence agency upon suspicions of planning to conduct political interference communication activities. According to a court document seen by INPACT, Shugaley and Sufyan were in charged of all the communications around the opening of the Russian House and were named by some people involved in the court case as the ones opening the cultural centre in Farcha. Accelerating openings as the best vector for deep influence and military engagement Russian Houses operate on most continents, from Europe to Africa through the American and Asian continents. The network appears to be in constant expansion in Africa, matching other influence offensives led by the Kremlin. In this aspect, the development of Russian Houses highlights the focus of the Russian influence strategy. New partnerships and future openings have been routinely announced between late 2024 and 2025, generally through already existing associations and centres. This is notably the case in Somalia and Sierra Leone, where the announcements were made by the Puchkin Centre and the Foundation for Russian Heritage respectively. Russian Houses have become the gravitational hubs around which pro- Kremlin media, influencers and proxy groups orbit, amplifying anti -Western narratives and embedding Moscow’s messaging within local communities. No longer mere echo chambers, these franchised outposts increasingly resemble recruitment halls for Russia’s war machine. This is a far cry from the traditional remit of cultural diplomacy. Institutions once meant to showcase Russia’s language, history and art have been recast, manipulated and milit arised— transformed from showcases of soft power into instruments of hard power.

1kc55W-eBk2Z2dMglQCk3joJTdZeEVhu3.text: GLOBAL KOMBAT The Russian one-stop shop for influence goes global MARCH 2024 1 BLUF ● The Pravda Network, initially identified by French disinformation watchdog Viginum, has recently expanded its scope of topics, targeted countries and audiences. This expansion places it as one of the most virulent, rapidly evolving informational threats against Europe. ● This network of domains and subdomains currently targets audiences based on country, language, organisation, or even based on personalities. Dedicated domains for example target United States President Donald Trump, German Chancellor Olaf Sholz or French President Emmanuel Macron. ● The network primarily targets Western audiences, in the United States, France, Germany, United Kingdom. Websites in English, French and German are the most active. With the newest expansion of the network to linguistic minorities in Europe, the Pravda network may attempt to create divisions with the targeted audiences. ● As highlighted by several publications, and confirmed by our technical analysis and collection of over 122,000 articles published by the network, the Pravda Network is a content aggregator sourcing its published content from third party sources. Its objective is to be a one-stop shop of Russian curated content for influence. ● Primary sources of content are Russian mainstream and influence media outlets (TASS, Russia Today), but also more “aggressive” news houses such as Tsargrad TV, owned by oligarch Konstantin Malofeev, or Newsfront, a website associated with the FSB. ● The surprisingly important volume of content shared from Tsargrad and NewsFront, which feature within the top 15 sources of content, immediately after major Russian media such as TASS, Russia Today or RIA Novosti, may indicate the involvement of either Konstantin Malofeev and/or the FSB behind the Pravda Network. There is very little explanation over why these websites feature so prominently, whilst similar and more prolific sources of content are available (Oriental Review, New Eastern Outlook…). 2 ● The majority of the sources of content are Telegram channels. The effectiveness of the Pravda Network’s own Telegram channels appears extremely limited. ● Previously identified disinformation and influence networks, such as African Initiative or InfoDefense, are regularly used as news sources by the Pravda Network. ● Pravda network also sources content from foreign citizens supporting Russia. The ecosystem is composed of pro-Russian supporters, but also more sophisticated influence channels such as France’s Stratpol. ● The Pravda network is hosted through Content Delivery Network provider CloudFlare, one of the world’s largest. Should legal actions and sanctions be formally taken against the network, it will force the migration of their hosted services to a new service provider, leading to some degree of at least temporary disruption. 3 CONTENT BLUF 2 CONTENT 4 SETTING THE SCENE 5 HOSTING INFRASTRUCTURE 6 RESEARCH METHODOLOGY 6 PRAVDA NETWORK GOES GLOBAL 7 MODUS OPERANDI 9 Most active domains 10 Most common sources 11 NETWORKS OF INTEREST 13 InfoDefense 14 African Initiative 20 EXAMPLES OF TARGET COUNTRIES 22 African continent coverage 22 United States coverage 25 France coverage 28 ANNEXES 31 Annex 1: list of Pravda network subdomains 31 Annex 2: IP address log of the Pravda network domains/subdomains. 34 4 SETTING THE SCENE French disinformation watchdog Viginum 1 published on 12th February 2024 a technical investigation into a network of Russian websites delivering packaged disinformation across Europe, the United Kingdom and the United States. This network, at the time composed of 193 websites, includes the “Pravda Ecosystem”, a network of websites, each dedicated to targeting specific countries. According to Viginum, the network has been associated with a digital marketing agency called TigerWeb and run by Evgeni SHEVTCHENKO, a 37 years old Crimea based man who has previously worked for Krimtechnology, Yandex and the Ministry of Labor and Social Protection of the Republic of Crimea. Evgeny Shevtchenko’s photo retrieved from Russian database and social media The company is specialised in designing websites and administering them, working mainly for businesses based in Crimea according to Tigerweb’s website. According to Viginum, Tigerweb has been creating and administering part of the Portal Kombat’s network. 1 https://www.sgdsn.gouv.fr/files/files/20240212_NP_SGDSN_VIGINUM_PORTAL-KOMBAT-NETWORK_ENG_VF.pdf 5 Tigerweb website Since then, the network appears to have grown in a significant manner, developing sub-domains dedicated to niche audiences such as Wales, Abkhazia, Catalan or Basque-speaking publics. Recently published investigations by CheckFirst 2 , the DFRLab 3 and the American Sunlight Project 4 have also detailed this recent expansion of the targeting scope of the Pravda Network. HOSTING INFRASTRUCTURE The Pravda network is hosted through the same infrastructure. Two unique IP addresses have been identified: 104.21.62.172 and 172.67.137.144. Both addresses are managed by Content Delivery Network provider CloudFlare 5 and are used to host a multitude of other websites 6 . The complete list of subdomains and their associated IP address is available as an annex of this report. RESEARCH METHODOLOGY INpact have conducted an in-depth analysis of the Pravda Network. This analysis is based on the collection of over 122,000 articles (n = 122 631) published by the 90 subdomains of the Pravda Network, over a 15 days period. The collection of these articles was completed by the extraction of the source from which each article originated. Additional analysis included the identification of the names of personalities, countries and organisations most often recorded within the title and content of each collected article. 6 https://viewdns.info/reverseip/?host=news-pravda.com&t=1 5 https://viewdns.info/iphistory/?domain=news-pravda.com 4 https://static1.squarespace.com/static/6612cbdfd9a9ce56ef931004/t/67bf1de6429a912e3cbe8c83/1740578284208/PK+Report .pdf 3 https://dfrlab.org/2025/02/24/russia-pravda-network-expands-worldwide/ 2 https://checkfirst.network/pravda-network-worldwide-expansion-and-llm-wikipedia-pollution/ 6 PRAVDA NETWORK GOES GLOBAL At the time of writing, INPact has identified 90 sub-domains from the main https://news-pravda.com/ domain. These subdomains appear to be targeting countries (eg: Albania, France, Belgium...), people (eg: Macron, Scholz, Trump), regions (eg: Balkans), organisations (eg: NATO) or even languages (eg: Catalan, Basque...). The list of subdomains included below highlights the scale of the efforts and resources placed at the disposal of the Pravda Network team. This complete list of subdomains and their assigned country/organisation/language is available in the annex of this report. The majority of the subdomains target countries (76.9%), whilst almost equal shares target people, organisations and “global/regions” (2.2% to 3.3%). 14.3% target languages/ethnicities (Dutch, Basque, Catalan, German, Portuguese, Wallon, French…). 7 8 The breakdown of targets clearly indicates that Pravda Networks is an influence network of websites targeting the usual set of entities, countries and personalities assessed hostile towards Russian policy. Europe, the United Kingdom and the United States are the most frequently observed targets of Pravda subdomains. This focus on influencing European and Western audiences is further confirmed by the three personalities specifically targeted by individual subdomains: United States President Donald Trump, German Chancellor Olaf Sholz and French President Emmanuel Macron. In Africa, the network initially targeted countries where Russian influence was already well established, such as the Central African Republic, Mali, Burkina Faso. These were soon completed by Sudan, Mauritania, Egypt… The process of assigning a subdomain to a country and selecting “targets” is currently unidentified. The deployment of websites in local languages - Basque, Galician, Serbian, Catalan… - may indicate an attempt to develop audiences within linguistic minorities. Whilst further analysis will be required, it is assessed that language-specific websites may attempt to drive negative narratives against the nation States where linguistic specificities exist. MODUS OPERANDI None of the websites of the Pravda network generates its own unique content. All are instead content aggregators, collecting, translating, processing and publishing news articles from a variety of sources, ranging from Telegram channels to official Russian State-owned media outlets (RIA, TASS). What makes the Prava network unique is its capabilities to target 64 countries and organisations in multiple languages with a unique repository gathering all key Russian outlets and country focused Telegram channels. The Pravda network also has deployed a multitude of Telegram channels in support of each subdomain. None of these channels appears to be particularly popular, judging by the very low number of subscribe 9 INPact have collected over 122 000 individual articles published by every subdomain of the Pravda network. Through this analysis, we have been able to identify with accuracy which are the top sources of content used by Pravda edit Subdomains 90 Unique sources 1,920 Domain type Number of domains Number of articles % of total Organisation-specific domains 2 3770 3% People-specific domains 3 9581 7.8% Country-specific domains 72 87 620 71% Language-specific domains 13 21 660 18.2% Most active domains The Pravda Network focuses production primarily against English-speaking audiences, and most notably against the United States. Whilst the domain name associated with English is logically the most active, it is immediately followed by the ones associated with the United States (https://usa.news-pravda.com) and United States President Donald Trump (https://trump.news-pravda.com). Whilst the domain name associated with the American President comes out as being the third most active, it is worthy to note that this domain recycles most of the content already published by the United States domain. Domain Domain designation Number of articles https://en.news-pravda.com English (language) 7180 https://usa.news-pravda.com USA 6900 https://trump.news-pravda.com U.S. President Donald Trump 6020 https://fr.news-pravda.com French (language) 5970 https://de.news-pravda.com Deutsch (language) 5350 https://rs.news-pravda.com Serbia 5340 https://es.news-pravda.com Spain 5220 https://france.news-pravda.com France 4460 https://lt.news-pravda.com Lithuania 4000 https://uk.news-pravda.com United Kingdom 3800 https://cz.news-pravda.com Czech Republic 3640 10 https://md.news-pravda.com Macedonia 3590 https://scholz.news-pravda.com German ChancellorOlaf Scholz 3490 https://ua.news-pravda.com Ukraine 3460 https://eu.news-pravda.com European Union 3370 https://nl.news-pravda.com Netherlands 2920 The list of the most active domains clearly point to a deliberate attempt to influence Western audiences.English, French and German-speaking audiences are the most often targeted. Most common sources Top 10 sources of content of the News Pravda network. Source: INpact data The top ten most commonly observed sources of content, across the range of subdomains, includes the most famous Russian media outlets, including TASS, RIA Novosti, Russia Today or Lenta. Source Number of articles TASS 4620 RIA Novosti 3880 Lenta 3630 Komsomolskaya Pravda 2790 Russia Today 2700 11 Russia Today (Arabic) 1710 Telegram “Llord of War” 1700 Tsargrad TV 1660 Telegram “Lovmokaa” 1470 Ren TV 1390 Telegram “Gek_genoeg” 1260 Telegram “Russia Today French” 1150 Telegram “Leblokrizov” 1090 Telegram “Denazi_UA” 1070 Telegram “SolovievLive” 1040 News-Front.su 988 Telegram “MGzpravy” 974 Ukraina.ru 937 Interestingly, two of the top websites used as content sources by the network are linked to a Russian oligarch, Konstantin Malofeev, and to the FSB. TsarGrad is a media owned by oligarch Konstantin Malofeev 7 . The media offers televisual and online content through multiple social media pages. One of the directors of the station is no one else than Alexander Dugin. NewsFront.su is a website associated with an organisation in Crimea, under United States sanctions 8 . Newsfront is reportedly linked to the Russian FSB. The organisation is staff by at least 70 journalists, freelancers and local editors based in Russia and Europe 9 . It is thought to be headed by Constantine Knyrik and aggressively pushes out disinformation and biased content. Along these sources appear a variety of Telegram channels, which constitute the majority of the sources from which the Pravda Network sources its content. 9 https://vsquare.org/newsfront-russia-hungary-disinformation-telegram-propaganda/ 8 https://home.treasury.gov/news/press-releases/jy0628 7 https://www.ft.com/content/27125702-71ec-11e5-ad6d-f4ed76f0900a 12 NETWORKS OF INTEREST Pravda Network acts as a content aggregator. The process of selecting news sources remains unknown, however the presence of very specific, remarkable sources such as Tsargrad TV and Newsfront may indicate the involvement of the sponsors of both media behind the Pravda Network. Telegram appears to be the main source of content, even though the top individual sources remain Russian influence media houses. 13 InfoDefense 14 The InfoDefense network is a known Russian disinformation/influence network. It was initially identified back in 2022, including by the Institute of Strategic Dialogue 10 or French journalist Nicolas Quénel, who infiltrated the network of stringers recruited and employed by InfoDefense 11 . The InfoDefense network is openly advertised on Pravda. So far, only publications by the InfoDefense Telegram channels have been used as sources by the Pravda Network. No articles were for example sourced from the French InfoDefense page (https://fr.infodefense.press/). The InfoDefense network of 26 Telegram channels is quoted by 53 domain names of the Pravda Network. The most active sources are Bulgaria, Portugal and Serbia. 11 https://www.numerama.com/cyberguerre/1553800-allo-paris-ici-moscou-les-coulisses-de-la-propagande-russe-reveles-dans-un e-enquete.html 10 https://www.isdglobal.org/digital_dispatches/the-info-defense-network-crowdsourcing-soldiers-for-the-information-war/ 15 Another noteworthy channel within the sources shared on News-Pravda.com, aimed at the French, is the Thomasovitch Telegram channel. To be more precise, this source is mentioned five times in a fortnight. INPACT focused on this pro-Russian propaganda relay because of the story of its administrator. 16 The individual, identified by our teams, comes from a department in central France. A student at a business school in 2022, he decided to study in Moscow to finish his degree. Interviewed by journalists at the time, he said he was afraid he wouldn't be able to return. Strange as it may seem, he is now a pro-Russian influencer operating from Moscow. Through various channels, he offers travel deals to Russia, while praising Vladimir Putin. He has even gone so far as to set up his own official travel agency on Russian soil. The target enjoys a network of French-speaking partners who act as facilitators for entering Russia, particularly via Kazan. INPACT takes a closer look at this network of individuals, all linked to each other via various pro-Russian propaganda groups and channels. Thomasovich’s main website, https://www.thomasovitch.com/, provides backlinks to many already identified Russian influence and disinformation operations targeting France, including the notorious Stratpol. 17 18 Thomasovich’s presence is not limited to the online realms. He also operates at least one travel agency in Russia. Thomasovich’s travel agency online presence. Source: OSINTTRACKER/INpact 19 African Initiative 20 The African Initiative network of Telegram channels is regularly used as sources by the Pravda Network. Similarly to what was observed with Info Defense, the African Initiative websites (https://afrinz.ru) does not feature in the list of sources of the Pravda Network, which seems to favour Telegram channels - likely for technical reasons, since collecting content off Telegram is much easier than from websites. Four Telegram channels are used as sources, “africaninitiative” (164 articles), “africaninitiativefr” (94 articles), “africaninitiativeen” (18 articles) and “africaninitiativept” (15 articles). These few sources are however used by 41 different domain names: 21 EXAMPLES OF TARGET COUNTRIES African continent coverage 16 domain names target African audiences. These domains source their content from 350 sources, some of which previously associated with influence operations by Prigozhin and Wagner networks. Across the 16 domain names targeting African audiences, the top sources from which content originates are essentially Russian influence media (Russia Today, Sputnik, RIA Novosti), national media houses (Agence d’Information du Burkina - AIB, Sarabic.ae, Minute.bf) or Telegram channels. 22 As expected, the Pravda network is heavily promoting Russian-African relationships and reinforcing messages against the West on neo-colonialism. It is also promoting its own initiatives like the Alabuga Start programme, a recruitment programme launched in the so-called Global South to recruit employees for the Alabuga Economic Zone where part of the militaro-industrial complex has been located. 23 Various screen shot from Western African Pravda networks 24 United States coverage 25 The United States, and associated domain name https://trump.news-pravda.com are covered by no less than 353 sources, totalling almost 13,000 articles. The most active sources are the Lenta and RIA Novosti media, rapidly followed by Tsargrad, SolovievLive and a multitude of Telegram channels. Combined together, all the sources used for the US domains of the Pravda network focus on glorifying Trump, the position of the US on the war in Ukraine. Articles aggregated by the network emphasise USA alignment to Russia, USA inferiority, praise Trump and criticise unacceptable positions like the transfer of Zaporizhzhya nuclear plant to Ukraine. 26 Various screen shot from the US and Trump Pravda networks 27 France coverage 28 Number of articles 4660 Number of sources 297 Sources (sample) Number of articles Telegram “Russia Today French” 634 Tass.ru 229 RIA Novosti 227 Lenta 206 Kirov Pravda 159 Russia Today 147 Telegram “VBachir” 135 Telegram “TASS_Europe” 108 Tsargrad TV 100 Ren TV 83 Russia Today French 83 Ukraina.ru 79 Telegram “Lomovkaa” 72 Newsfront 65 Telegram “BorisKarpovRussie” 59 Telegram “MondePourTous” 45 Telegram “Russosphere” 44 NewsFront French 30 Telegram “Chronique des conflits” 29 Telegram “infodefFRANCE&” 28 Telegram “internationalreporters” 26 Telegram “engountchibehanzinTV” 15 Telegram “infodefenseFRreserve” 10 29 The French Pravda network is focusing on the typical narratives promoted by Russia as a one stop shop for anti-French influence: the decline of France domestically due to immigration and internationally as well as its position in the war in Ukraine and relation to Russia. The bad French translation is also to be noted for all the Pravda francophone networks. 30 ANNEXES Annex 1: list of Pravda network subdomains Domain name Targeted Country Target Target Type
